title: Baby's hospital death caused by congenital heart condition, inquest finds
text:
The death of a baby girl at a south-east Queensland hospital was the result of her undiagnosed congenital heart defect, "not the standard of her post-delivery care", a coroner has ruled.

Thea Flaskett died four hours after she was born via vacuum assisted delivery at Redcliffe Hospital in September 2023.

An autopsy found the newborn had the undetected cardiac abnormality Transposition of the Great Arteries (TGA).

The circumstances surrounding her death were the subject of an inquest held in Brisbane last year after her parents raised concerns about her care post-birth.

Thea's parents James and Meg Flaskett raised concerns about her care at Redcliffe Hospital. (AAP: Jono Searle)

On Monday, Deputy State Coroner Stephanie Gallagher published her findings and ruled Thea's cause of death was TGA and "one or more factors" made her condition unsurvivable.

"It was compounded by poorer oxygenation of the blood than most other babies born with that condition would experience … because Thea had reduced shunting and possibly postpartum haemorrhage," she said in her findings.

When determining whether TGA should have been picked up in scans during her mother's pregnancy, Ms Gallagher found that was "not reasonably supported by the evidence".

Missed detection 'not uncommon'

Ms Gallagher said experts called during the inquest explained antenatal detection rates for the condition were low.

"Missed detection, such as in Thea's case, is not uncommon," she said in her findings.

" That it was missed here was through no fault of the sonographers or radiologist involved in Thea's care. "

Ms Gallagher said the fact it went undetected was "very clearly a consequence of the known limitations of antenatal screening".

"The scanning was done in a conventional way and adhered to the prescribed methods, protocols and guidelines, and the imaging was of such quality that rescanning was not required."

The inquest heard when Thea began to deteriorate in the minutes after her birth, staff at the hospital attempted resuscitation.

Staff 'did everything they could'

A neonatal rescue team based at the Royal Brisbane and Women's Hospital (RBWH) also attended and were involved in specialist care.

Ms Gallagher found all staff "did everything they could, and ought to have done, to try to save Thea's life".

"Despite resuscitating Thea to a high standard, her outcome was determined by her underlying congenital cardiac abnormality — not the standard of her post-delivery care," she said in her findings.

Thea died just four hours after she was born in September 2023. (Supplied)

Ultimately, Ms Gallagher found if her condition had been detected antenatally, the outcome may have been the same.

"There was no guarantee that Thea's death would have been avoided," the coroner said in her findings.

Two recommendations were made, including that Metro North Hospital and Health services (MNHHS) and the NeoRESQ team at the RBWH make inquiries about the resources used in New South Wales public hospitals, in relation to live-streaming to consultants.

"In paediatric resuscitation bays in hospitals they serve to permit adequate visualisation of the patient and attending staff, as well as oral communication," Ms Gallagher said in her findings.

"I also recommend that NeoRESQ and MNHHS enquire as to the detail required to be completed on the intake form for the like neonatal retrieval service in NSW, to see if amendments to the form used in Queensland would be apt."

title: Tears streaming, Messi departs the World Cup stage a legend
text:
Lionel Messi had so desperately hoped his World Cup would end differently.

Tears running down his cheeks, a silver medal around his neck, his teammates in various states of depression, strewn around him like the detritus of some cataclysmic event as Spain roared amid garish golden confetti and an explosion of colour and joy.

Arguably the greatest player to ever grace a pitch was distraught.

It's impossible to know exactly what was going through his mind.

A decade earlier, on the same Metlife Stadium pitch where he was currently sobbing, Messi had tearfully decided to retire from international football after losing the Copa América final against enormous underdogs Chile.

The pain of that defeat was simply too much to bear for Messi, saddled with the expectation of a nation desperate to anoint him as Maradona's heir.

The world was the beneficiary as Messi reversed that decision.

Fans around the world have rejoiced at Messi's success. (Getty Images: Wang HE)

So too were Argentina, who claimed the 2021 Copa América title, the 2022 Finalissima and, most tellingly, the 2022 World Cup under Messi's leadership, cementing his legacy among his fellow countrymen for all time.

But once again in the New Jersey sunshine, Messi's tears were the defining feature for a generation of football fans.

Was this another tearful goodbye to the international stage?

Messi is 39 years old.

With 19-year-olds Lamine Yamal and Pau Cubarsí on the other side of the divide and winning for Spain, the next generation is not just arriving to force him out but has already arrived.

To play another World Cup in four years may be beyond even his mystic powers, even despite his impressive ability to adapt to the constraints his age has imposed on him.

The next generation, personified by Lamine Yamal, is snapping at Messi's aging heels. (Getty Images: PA Images/Bradley Collyer)

At this World Cup, Messi has walked for roughly half the time he was on the pitch, strolling and stalking for about 5 kilometres per game.

And yet, inarguably, no one player has had a greater role in his team's success than Messi has for Argentina.

His goals, eight of them, and his four assists dragged the defending champions through the group stage and the knockout rounds.

His ability to bend a game to his will, no matter the situation, made him seem nothing short of inevitable.

Despite all the walking, whenever Argentina threatened, the little magician was there in the heart of the action, conjuring and scheming and dragging his team home just when they needed him to.

His very presence seemed to inspire his teammates to feats beyond their capabilities, a totemic figure to rally around, his energy imbuing those around him with positivity and confidence and class.

Lionel Messi came close to lifting the World Cup for the second time. (Getty Images: FIFA/Tullio Puglia)

Against England in the semifinal, his two assists powered the Albiceleste to a famous victory in stoppage time.

Against Egypt, as his team faced elimination at 2-0 down, he provided the cross for the first and delivered the shot for their equaliser.

Against Spain in the final? It just didn't happen.

The fact that his tears only came after he had been given his silver medal was almost illustrative: No matter how late it got, Messi perhaps still had hope that he might somehow manage to influence the game in his favour.

He'd done it before, after all.

Only when Donald Trump laid the heavy silver slice of defeat around his neck did he realise there would be no more magic, that no amount of sorcery from his phenomenal feet could change history now.

Against Spain, the little master managed just 55 touches, none of which came in the Spain penalty area.

Lionel Messi was unable to stamp his authority on the contest. (Getty Images: FIFA/Maja Hitij)

He was stifled as effectively as if Spain wrapped him in a blanket.

And his Argentina teammates simply couldn't back up their superstar when it mattered most.

Messi could not deliver the magical moment that his nation demanded.

It was telling that Argentina offered so little other than niggly fouls and angry words as opposed to the free-flowing football Spain occasionally managed when the mood took them.

Let's not beat around the bush: Argentina were woeful in this final.

Argentina didn't have a single shot on goal in the first 90 minutes of the game, the first time any team has failed to do so in a World Cup final.

Their first attempt on goal did not come until the 26th minute of extra time.

Lionel Messi had Argentina's first shot of the game, in the second half of extra time. (Getty Images: FIFA/Maja Hitij)

Their 25 total fouls resulted in seven yellow cards, one of which was a second that saw Enzo Fernández up-end Pau Cubarsí, making him the sixth player ever to be dismissed in a World Cup final.

Argentina's nefarious skulduggery was finally, belatedly, penalised by a referee brave enough not to stand for it.

Coach Lionel Scaloni said afterwards that his team "showed that we know how to lose" in his post-match press conference, but nothing could be further from the truth.

The Argentines thoroughly disgraced themselves after the game's conclusion, Leandro Paredes lashing out and sparking a minor brawl that ended with him shown a red card after the final whistle.

Fractious barely covers it.

If this is to be his international swan song, the landscape post-Messi may as well be post-apocalyptic, so absent of joy and light does it appear to hold.

Claudio Tapia, the president of the Argentina Football Association, knows how important Messi is to the team. (Getty Images: Andrew Harnik)

It was not Messi's fault that he could not have the impact on the game he might have hoped for.

Spain were able to successfully nullify a far better team than Argentina in the semifinal with their Rodri-led midfield dominance suffocating their opponents' creativity.

Messi simply didn't have the space.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

He was closed down as ominously and relentlessly as the ticking of the clock that will one day herald the end of his stellar career.

Messi couldn't muster the magic to help Argentina become the third men's team to win back-to-back World Cups.

It is not the finale that Messi might have envisaged.

But if this is to be his tearful sign off, fans will still look back at having been blessed by one of football's greatest of all time.

title: Key takeaways from random FIFA World Cup half-time show
text:
It was a first for the FIFA World Cup: a half-time show, introduced in 2026, with a line-up of global superstars that included Madonna, Justin Bieber, Shakira, Burna Boy and BTS.

Australian singer-songwriter Emmanuel Kelly, who rose to fame with his performances on The X Factor, was also in the mix.

Some enthusiastically welcomed the show, but it had football tragics, who aren't used to such a spectacle during "the beautiful game", collectively pulling out their red cards.

Former Manchester United and England legend Wayne Rooney, who is widely celebrated as one of the greatest players of his generation, even offered a brief but brutal assessment live on the BBC.

"I like a lot of them artists, but I thought it was crap," Rooney said.

World Cup final quick hits Argentina's players lash out in defeat, Ferran Torres becomes a Spanish icon forever and Donald Trump attempts to crash the winners' party. Here are the World Cup final quick hits.

"It just didn't get me going and I wanted the football to come back on … I like Burna Boy, I like Bieber, I like Shakira, but that was too flat."

Ouch.

But this article is for the pop culture-types.

Here are some key takeaways from the inaugural 2026 FIFA World Cup half-time show, which took place at the temporarily renamed New York New Jersey stadium.

Madonna … Ronaldo and Ronaldinho

Madonna kicked things off with her track Music from her 2000 album of the same name.

The song, with the refrain: "music makes the people come together", had a twist to it, infused with elements of Disco Inferno, which was made famous by The Trammps in 1976, and was the centrepiece of the 1977 Saturday Night Fever soundtrack.

The mashup also included elements of the song Danceteria from Madonna's chart-topping new album Confessions II.

The Queen of Pop made her entrance in a dune buggy driven by surprise guests, Brazilian football legends Ronaldo and Ronaldinho.

BTS truncated but flawless

K-pop superstars BTS were decked out in leather, and their dance moves came with the kind of kicks that certainly weren't out of place on a football pitch.

BTS brought the moves. (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters: Vincent Carchietta)

They performed their global hit Dynamite in a performance that felt too brief.

But that's because the entire halftime show had an 11-minute time limit, exceeding this by only about 30 seconds.

Justin Bieber introduced by Ted Lasso

Actors Jason Sudeikis and Brendan Hunt, in character as Ted Lasso and Coach Beard respectively, made a surprise appearance to introduce Justin Bieber.

Justin Bieber brought down the tempo. (REUTERS: Amanda Perobelli)

Bieber lowered the energy considerably by launching into Everything Hallelujah from his 2025 album Swag II.

The acoustic performance had Bieber strumming a guitar.

The minimalist stripped-backed performance felt intimate, and therefore a little out of place.

Shakira and Burna Boy perform official song

Shakira was a crowd favourite. ( IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters: Mark J Rebilas)

Colombian superstar Shakira and African giant Burna Boy performed the official FIFA World Cup song Dai Dai.

They were surrounded by Uganda's Ghetto Kids, a renowned dance and music troupe founded by a former maths teacher.

This performance brought the tempo way back up, jarring following Bieber's set, but a crowd favourite according to ABC Sport.

Coldplay, the Muppets, and an Aussie

The most random part of the whole show had to be The Muppets' Electric Mayhem performing a rendition of Seven Nation Army.

They were joined by Iranian violinist Bijan Mortazavi and an orchestra of musicians from the New York Philharmonic and Venezuela's Simón Bolívar Symphony Orchestra led by Gustavo Dudamel.

The Sesame Street flavour continued when Muppets from the show joined Disney's The Muppets to debut We Dance, an original song written by Coldplay for the occasion.

Coldplay was there too, including frontman Chris Martin, who curated the half-time show.

The PS22 Chorus performed too, with a cameo from Adriana Lima.

Australian Emmanuel Kelly, who appeared on season three of The X Factor, where he shared what it was like to live with a disability, was also part of the set.

A prior taste of Hollywood

The FIFA World Cup final began with a taste of Hollywood with Tinseltown superstar Tom Cruise delivering an inspirational monologue.

"Football is a language spoken without words, a force that unites people, turns strangers into friends, and reminds us of what we all have in common," Cruise said.

Robbie Williams performed official anthem Desire alongside Italian pop singer Laura Pausini and lead singer of the Pussycat Dolls Nicole Scherzinger.

Singer, actress and talk show host Jennifer Hudson sang The Star-Spangled Banner.

title: The 13 photos that summed up the chaos of the 2026 World Cup final
text:
It was a tournament where the football was magical, the off-field antics were questionable, and ultimately, Spain left the United States as the champions of the world.

A day after the utter chaos of the third-placed playoff between France and England, where 10 goals were scored, Argentina and Spain played things a little more conservatively — right up until the game exploded into action.

Here are the 13 photos that tell the story of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final.

1. The Fresh Prince of Barcelona

Teenage superstar Lamine Yamal arrives at the stadium looking like the coolest dude on earth. (Getty Images: Evrim Aydin/Anadolu)

While much of the tournament had been focused on the more established superstar names like Lionel Messi, Erling Haaland and Kylian Mbappé, teenager Lamine Yamal had floated under the radar for the World Cup passers-by.

Coming to the final having never lost a match where he had started for Spain, expectations were high on the 19-year-old as he came into the stadium. Would he be the hero that Spain needed to overcome the perceived might of Argentina?

Whatever the pressure, he arrived looking like the coolest dude on the planet, calm, collected and rocking a style straight out of The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air.

2. Smoke and mirrors

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney greets US President Donald Trump ahead of the World Cup final. (Getty Images: Eva Marie Uzcategui)

All eyes turned to the skies before kick-off as US President Donald Trump made his usual subtle entrance into the area, coming in low and fast in the Marine One helicopter.

Once settled in prime position in a corporate box on the wings, Trump was afforded the questionable chance to give the trophy a little pat as FIFA boss Gianni Infantino brought the iconic gold statue to Trump's seat.

Before that, though, there was the potentially awkward meeting between Trump and Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, just a couple of days after Trump accused Canada of being "filthy" and "polluted" over its wildfire smoke that had blanketed New York in the lead-up to the tournament.

All-in-all things seemed reasonably diplomatic, given Mother Nature had done her job to send the smoke packing just in time for the game.

3. America makes a double Buff

American announcer Bruce Buffer turns the World Cup into a blood sport briefly with his iconic introduction. (Getty Images: Sebastian Widmann)

In the most American moment of this entire game, event organisers decided that what this little old soccer match needed was a double Buffer.

Bruce and Michael Buffer, to be exact, the legendary ring announcers from every blood sport imaginable.

Flipping from one brother to the next, the pair excitedly announced that the game was "ready to rumble", in scenes not far off that time that soccer came to Springfield in The Simpsons.

4. The GOAT meets the rising star

Lionel Messi and Lamine Yamal share a laugh together early in the match. (Getty Images: Hannah Peters)

Looking a little less cool than when he arrived, but still the picture of confidence, Yamal made a beeline for Lionel Messi before the match to wish arguably the greatest player in history some luck.

It was a full circle moment for the pair, who originally met on a photo shoot in 2007 when Yamal's parents won a Unicef raffle to have Yamal photographed for a calendar with the then rising star Messi.

This time around Messi's hair was a little shorter and his face a little more wearied, while Yamal was a little less nude.

5. The World Cup's Mariah Carey

Shakira performs during the halftime show, a spectacle one England footballing legend would go on to call "crap". (Getty Images: Lars Baron)

Shakira was rolled out for the halftime show, with the Colombian star fast becoming to the World Cup what Mariah Carey is to Christmas.

She's just always there.

While her performance was fantastic, the rest of it — from Justin Bieber to Madonna to BTS — left a little bit to be desired.

So much so that English footballing champion Wayne Rooney didn't hold back with what he thought.

"I thought it was crap," he said on the BBC coverage.

6. The spectacular red card

Argentina's Enzo Fernandez fouls Spain's Pau Cubarsi, leading to the Argentinian's second yellow card and a send-off. (Getty Images: Al Bello)

As soon as we all saw it happen on the TV, we knew that the photos were going to be spectacular.

And veteran Getty photographer Al Bello didn't let us down.

Rising Spanish star Pau Cubarsi was sent flying by Argentina's Enzo Fernandez, but it was made all the more spectacular by Cubarsi really leaning into the flip.

Had Fernandez not already had a yellow, it's unlikely he would have been sent off at all, but the red card made it all the more dramatic as the scores stood at 0-0 heading into the death.

7. Hydration break goes to a new level

Spain's Dani Olmo takes the hydration break to the extreme as he utilises the sprinklers to cool down. (Getty Images: Evrim Aydin/Anadolu)

Two things have been major talking points during this tournament — it's been bloody hot and the hydration breaks have been bloody irritating.

So it was fun to see the two come together in the final regular time break of the tournament, as the Spanish players took advantage of the sprinklers to cool off.

Was it the trick that won them the World Cup? Who's to say. But almost certainly yes.

8. Free kick wipes a goal

Nico Williams reacts after his goal was marked off for a foul that was immediately obvious on the replays afterwards. (Getty Images: Carl Recine)

In a moment of brilliance, Nico Williams looked to have won it for Spain when he slotted a goal past the keeper to make it 1-0 late.

But the referee called it back. There was a free kick somewhere in the mess of bodies in the box.

In live action it wasn't immediately clear where the free kick was. On replay, it still wasn't totally clear.

But on the fifth or sixth watch — yeah, no idea.

Williams was furious, and rightfully so.

9. Deadlock ends in headlock

Nico Williams gives Ferran Torres a little chokehold celebration after Torres scored the winning goal. (Getty Images: Nick Potts)

Go back to the very first photo at the top of this story and you can experience the majesty of the Ferran Torres goal that broke the game open.

Hair blowing in the breeze, his teammates running in behind him, the fans screaming and cheering in ecstasy.

And then Williams decided to choke Torres for some reason.

Angry that he wasn't the hero after his goal was wiped? An in-joke that we don't know about?

Who knows. But it made for an odd scene in an incredible moment.

10. Tears of a legend

Lionel Messi slumps to the ground after the final whistle, in what will likely be the football legend's last World Cup game. (Getty Images: Michael Regan)

Small in stature but grand in legacy, Messi cut a forlorn figure after the final whistle.

His tiny body huddled in the middle of the massive stadium was enough to make anyone feel sorry for the attacking superstar.

His place as the greatest player the game has ever seen would have been secured had he managed to drag his teammates to victory, but even Messi couldn't get his countrymen through the incredible defensive wall that Spain had built.

11. Infantino chases after Trump

Rodri prepares to lift trophy as Gianni Infantino attempts to move Donald Trump out of frame. (Getty Images: Dan Mullan)

Trump had made headlines this time last year when he joined Chelsea on stage for the celebrations in the Club World Cup final, despite the apparent confusion of the players.

This time there was surely no way that Trump would be caught right in the middle of the pack as the Spanish players lifted the most coveted trophy in world sport, right?

Well, kind of. This time Trump wandered right across stage as Infantino pivoted left, leaving the FIFA boss to scramble after the US president, with the pair awkwardly caught in the team celebration photos.

On the broadcast, you could hear someone say "they'll crop him out" in an unexpected, but funny, hot mic moment.

12. Utter heartache for Argentina

Argentina goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez shows his dejection as the ceremony for the winners gets into full swing. (Getty Images: Ryan Pierse)

Goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez had been immense through the World Cup.

But as Spain lifted the trophy, the Aston Villa keeper let the emotions flow, caught by the camera as he sat on the sidelines thinking about what might have been.

It was a reminder of what this thing, this silly, epic, immense mess of a game, means to the people who play it.

13. The love of a mum

Lamine Yamal's mum races out onto the field to celebrate the victory with her son. (Getty Images: John Todd)

In the end, all we really want is the hug of a mum who's proud of you.

And that's what Yamal got as his mum, Sheila, ran onto the ground to give her son a big old cuddle.

Having given birth at just 16 years old as an immigrant from Equatorial Guinea, Sheila's story is the one that really captures the essence of the World Cup.

Not the championship rings or corporate boxes. Not the powerful men in powerful suits making powerful political moves.

A mum with a dream for her son to shine on the world stage.

This was the moment that really mattered.

title: Bombers star cleared for return despite abrupt mid-game hospital visit
text:
Essendon's star forward Nate Caddy has been cleared to return to action after being hospitalised with an elevated heart rate.

Caddy was sent to hospital during the Bombers' win against GWS on Sunday but was discharged that night.

The 21-year-old is expected to train with his teammates this week and play against Hawthorn on Saturday.

"There was some concern in the first quarter when Nate's heart rate became elevated," Essendon's football boss Dan McPherson said on Monday.

"He was removed from the game and our medical team assessed him at the ground before sending him to hospital for further assessment.

"He was discharged last night and is back at home."

McPherson said Caddy, who recently signed a contract extension until the end of 2031, was expected to "train and play without concern this week".

AAP

title: Uranium export deal sparks talk of opportunities for Broken Hill
text:
The outback mining city of Broken Hill is well known for its silver, lead and zinc.

It hosts one of the world's largest ore bodies, after all.

The city is not known for its uranium, but a recent agreement to supply India for its nuclear energy industry has some questioning whether there could be hidden opportunity in the city.

The Minerals Council of Australia says the prospectivity for uranium around Broken Hill is strong.

But with a ban on uranium mining in New South Wales, and no identified ore bodies in the state, extraction could be a pipe dream.

The start of another mining boom?

When Jim Hickey first moved to Broken Hill in the 1970s, the mining city had a population of about 28,000.

Now, Cr Hickey is the deputy mayor and he has seen the city shrink to about 17,500 people.

Broken Hill has a rich mining history. (ABC News: Bill Ormonde)

The Broken Hill City Council is expecting an increase in population with the looming commencement of major mining and renewable projects, but Cr Hickey sees uranium as a chance to bring about another mining boom for the city.

"If the [uranium] ban was lifted … mining companies would come in and stake some claims," Cr Hickey said.

"They would know now exactly what the exploration has shown and where it would be.

" This would be another string to our bow and keep Broken Hill well and surely alive and increase our population back to a good-sized city again. "

With the export deal with India freshly signed, Cr Hickey said he believed pressure would start mounting on the NSW government to repeal the prohibition on mining uranium.

A bill to do just that has passed NSW parliament's upper house, but that is as far as it is likely to go given longstanding opposition by the Labor party.

NSW Resources Minister Courtney Houssos told the Legislative Council in February that Labor's position would not change.

She also said that while exploration for uranium was allowed, "there has been very little interest in uranium exploration in this state, and no licences have been granted".

Jim Hickey would like to see a ban on uranium mining lifted in NSW. (ABC Broken Hill: Bill Ormonde)

Cr Hickey is urging the government to reconsider, particularly in light of the economic opportunities.

"We pay a lot of royalties now and we'd be increasing our royalties to the government … [and] they need every cent they can get.

"I think it'd be very profitable to them."

Broken Hill Labor Party branch president and local councillor Blake Edgecumbe said he did not doubt there would be an economic boom should a ban be lifted, but believed it would need to be weighed on balance with environmental concerns.

"I'm sure there'd be a lot of discussions happening, state and federally," Cr Edgecumbe said.

"I know certain political parties and certain people of certain persuasions would be pushing for the affirmative, but I think it's important that we look at environmental impacts as well as economics."

Economic opportunity

The Minerals Council of Australia would like to see NSW, Western Australia and Queensland collectively repeal prohibitions on uranium mining.

CEO Tania Constable said even if all three of Australia's operating uranium mines were to expand, it would still not be enough to meet India's ambitious uranium targets.

"India is going to require around about 23,000 tonnes of uranium each year to get their 100 gigawatts of nuclear energy," Ms Constable said.

"That would almost double what we're currently producing, and they're going to still need so much more.

"If we get mines going in Western Australia, if we see more opportunities in South Australia or the Northern Territory, and indeed if prohibitions are lifted in NSW, there is real opportunity for our industry."

Tania Constable would like to see bans on uranium mining in NSW, WA and Queensland lifted. (ABC News: Phil Hemingway)

There is a growing demand for uranium globally as countries chase net-zero emissions targets, and Australia is a significant resource.

It is the world's fourth-largest uranium producer but holds almost a third of the world's total resources.

"There's plenty of opportunity to move the needs of the nuclear energy industry across the world, for India, for the United States, for France, for other countries that are looking to decarbonise their economies," Ms Constable said.

She also supports exploring uranium opportunities around Broken Hill.

"It's unlikely to be the deposit itself, but [Broken Hill] does have prospectivity given the location that it is in."

Not the frontline for uranium

Emeritus Professor and Hancock Prospecting executive director Ian Plimer said he expected any battle to repeal a uranium ban would take place outside of NSW.

"[It would be] in Western Australia and Queensland, both of which have got identified uranium deposits," Professor Plimer said.

While he agreed there was prospectivity around the outback city, he said it would not draw focus for governments and mining companies.

Uranium is often referred to as "yellow cake" because of its colour. (Reuters: Mariana Bazo)

"My geological reasoning would be that you would go west, you would actually deal with the material that has leached off a great pile of Broken Hill rocks and ended up in the Frome Basin.

"That's where you would have the traces of uranium that are found in Broken Hill, concentrated in salt lakes."

But where Broken Hill could reap some rewards is through an expansion of the Honeymoon Uranium Mine, 80 kilometres away in South Australia, which is supported by a workforce in Broken Hill.

"[The community would benefit] in terms of infrastructure, in terms of employment," he said.

title: Has AI changed your life in unexpected ways? The ABC wants to hear from you
text:
Most of us never chose to live in an AI world. Yet over the past few years, artificial intelligence (AI) has become part of everyday life, changing how we think, learn and communicate.

Across the Asia Pacific, countries are taking very different approaches. For example, Singapore is expanding AI into education and public services, while India is trialling AI in governance and local administration.

'The future is happening without us' We asked readers if they had thoughts or concerns about the rise of AI. This is what you told us.

We want to understand what these changes look like from your perspective. So if you have a view, experience or concern, we want to hear from you via the form below.

We're especially interested in views that don't usually make headlines: the unofficial, unexpected or creative ways AI is being adopted in everyday life, particularly in key institutions where trust, expertise and accountability matter most.

Do you think it has potential to improve education, the legal system or governance? Or are you concerned the risks are unknown or too high? Has it changed dynamics at home? Or who family members turn to first for advice or information?

Your responses will help inform our reporting on how Australia compares to countries going through similar changes.

Please be assured any personal information you provide will be treated as strictly confidential and the ABC will not publish any personal details that may identify you without getting your permission.

Loading...

title: L7 bassist Jennifer Finch dies days after revealing cancer diagnosis
text:
Jennifer Finch, the longtime bassist for influential grunge rock group L7, has died.

Finch, who had been diagnosed with thyroid cancer in 2011, revealed she was diagnosed with an aggressive form of brain cancer last week. She was 59.

L7, who last toured Australia as part of the 2024 Good Things festival, are closely linked with the grunge and riot grrrl movements, creating space in the late 80s and 1990s for women in male-dominated punk and rock scenes.

Finch's bandmates shared the news of her death on Saturday on Instagram, calling her a "beloved bandmate, friend, and fellow troublemaker".

"She had a long courageous fight with brain cancer and was loved by many wonderful friends, musical peers and fans worldwide," wrote L7's Donita Sparks, Suzi Gardner and Demetra "Dee" Plakas.

Loading Instagram content

Finch was due to join L7 on the feminist rock band's The Last Hurrah farewell tour this year, however, she pulled out last week after revealing her brain cancer diagnosis, giving the band her blessing to forge ahead.

A GoFundMe campaign to help pay for her treatment, launched by friends and family, swiftly surpassed its goal and raised nearly $US400,000. Despite initial radiation treatment, "unforeseen complications" led to Finch enduring "multiple surgeries and a string of difficult setbacks".

Loading Instagram content

Born August 5, 1966, and raised by her adoptive parents in Los Angeles, Finch had a passion for photography, often documenting the city's burgeoning punk and alt-rock scenes, snapping influential groups like The Cramps and Bad Religion.

In the early 80s, Finch played bass in Sugar Babydoll, led by future Hole frontwoman Courtney Love, and The Pandoras.

Finch joined L7 in 1986, a year after Donita Sparks and Suzi Gardner formed the band. They released their self-titled debut in 1988 via influential independent record label Epitaph.

After adding Dee Plakas on drums, the group's 1990 album Smell The Magic made them one of the few non-Seattle bands signed to Sub Pop and earliest all-female groups in the grunge explosion. Finch even dated Nirvana drummer Dave Grohl for a time.

L7 blazed a trail for women in grunge – they just wanted to rock "We've all got terrible flaws as musicians," frontwoman Donita Sparks says. "But when we come together, it sounds great

Their 1992 album Bricks Are Heavy is considered one of the era's most significant, featuring L7's biggest crossover hit Pretend We're Dead, a searing political commentary on the marginalisation of women in society inserted into a hooky anthem.

Fiercely rejecting traditional beauty standards and fearlessly advocating for women's rights, L7 co-founded Rock for Choice in 1991, a concert series raising funds and awareness for reproductive rights.

Four women playing loud, aggressive tunes confronting sexism and political disillusion was considered a feminist statement in and of itself. But L7 did not actively try to be a feminist band.

"We don't write in any contrived way," Sparks told Double J in 2019. "We write from very personal experiences and they just morph into something else.

"We're not writing feminist lyrics. We're writing L7 lyrics and I think they speak to everybody."

Why L7 want you to tap into your inner teenager Donita Sparks on feminism in grunge, wild mosh pits and why she feels like a superhero

In 1996, two years after their fourth album Hungry for Stink, Finch left the band and played in other groups, namely OtherStarPeople and The Shocker, and launched her own label, Little Pusher Records.

Following an acrimonious split in 2001, L7 reunited in 2014 and Finch rejoined, playing on their 2019 reunion album Scatter The Rats.

The band then toured regularly, playing headline shows and festivals throughout the US, UK and Australia where, in 2023, they performed Bricks Are Heavy in full on tour.

Among those in the music industry mourning Finch's death are Courtney Love, Kathryn Valentine of the Go-Go's, Lunachicks and Garbage.

title: Reese accepts Brondello's apology over 'protected species' comment
text:
Angel Reese has accepted Australian coach Sandy Brondello's apology for an inappropriate comment about the Atlanta Dream star.

The WNBA suspended Brondello for one game after she was overheard calling Reese a "protected species" during the Dream's 111-92 win over Brondello's Toronto Tempo at the weekend.

"First, I appreciate Sandy's apology, but I also appreciate the love and support that I've had for the last 48 hours," Reese said before the Dream played the Chicago Sky on Monday (AEST).

"It has been very overwhelming with a lot of love and also other things. But I am grateful that the league did take action."

Brondello's comment about Reese was picked up by the broadcast after Toronto player Nyara Sabally went down with an injury and was called for a foul following contact with Reese.

Brondello apologised on social media and Reese posted "ARE WE SURPRISED?!" after the game, tagging Brondello's account, followed by a clown face emoji.

The "protected species" phrase is used in Australian sports to express frustration for a lack of calls against a player. However, in the US, the phrase suggests that someone isn't human and could be seen as derogatory toward Black people.

"There is no place for discrimination or hate in this league from anyone — from fans to coaches to players," Reese said.

"I'm just grateful that we are able to move on from the situation and just play winning basketball. The WNBA is supposed to be positive and we are going in a great light right now, so I just want to continue that and move forward."

AP

title: Quick hits: Trump crashes Spain's party, Argentina lose heads in defeat
text:
Argentina's players lash out in defeat, Ferran Torres becomes a Spanish icon forever, and Donald Trump attempts to crash the winners' party.

Here are today's quick hits.

1. Ferran Torres. Remember the name

The surest way to ensure your name lives forever in the annals of football history is to score the winner in a World Cup final. With a brilliantly struck half-volley in the 106th minute of the 2026 final, Spain's Ferran Torres joined that illustrious group.

Spain had dominated the game. It had ridden the many, many challenges of an Argentina side hell-bent on destroying the match's flow, but had still been taken deep into extra time without a breakthrough.

Ferran Torres celebrates scoring the winning goal in a World Cup final. (Getty Images: David Ramos)

Lamine Yamal nearly scored from a free-kick in regulation. Nico Williams thought he had scored, before the referee's whistle intervened. Mikel Merino should have scored moments later, but his free header blazed wide.

But in the end, it was all just in service of Torres's magic moment. Williams did brilliantly to keep a cross alive with a clever header back into a dangerous spot. Without the requirement of a settling touch, Torres lifted the killer blow above Argentina's keeper, Emi Martinez.

An injury time winner settled Spain's first World Cup title, Andres Iniesta the provider in 2010. He now has company in Spanish folklore. Ferran Torres, a legend forever.

Loading...

2. Disgraceful Argentinian head-loss in defeat

There are ways to win, and there are ways to lose. Argentina had already tread the line of the former this World Cup, but blew any notion of sportsmanship out of the water in the final defeat.

As Spain's players ran onto the field in celebration at the final whistle, Argentina's Nahuel Molina threw a punch to the chest of Spanish midfielder Rodri.

Rodri turned, reacting with surprise and anger, and from there, a scrap broke out.

Leandro Paredes lashed out Gavi after full-time. (Getty Images: Marvin Ibo Guengoer)

Argentina's Leandro Paredes, who had already been yellow carded in the game for pushing a player over in an earlier stink, completely lost his head.

Paredes went after Spain's Gavi, grabbing him by the neck and throwing him to the ground.

Paredes was shown a red card after the match had finished for his outburst, while Argentina's manager Lionel Scaloni had to run onto the field to break up the melee.

A disappointing exit for Argentina on an overall disappointing day.

3. Wild challenge and red card cost Argentina

Paredes was not the first Argentinian to see red, and indeed the game's first dismissal had far more bearing on the result.

Enzo Fernández was shown a second yellow and then a red just before the end of regulation time, forcing Argentina to try to hold on with 10 men through the 30 minutes to come.

Pau Cubarsi goes flying after Enzo Fernandez's tackle.

Fernandez's first booking was seemingly for dissent, as he vociferously disagreed with a refereeing decision, but his second was an outrageously dumb and reckless challenge.

With a 50-50 there to be won, Spain's Pau Cubarsi got to the ball a split second before Fernandez, but the Argentine made no effort at all to avoid the contact he was about to create. He leapt into the challenge, sending Cubarsi flying head over heels through the air.

Fernández again tried to argue the point with the referee, but there was no chance at all of a reprieve. A moment of recklessness had taken Argentina's precarious position and made it somehow even worse.

4. Messi can't rescue a dismal performance

Lionel Messi could not hold back the tears at the full-time whistle, or indeed after he had received his runners-up medal. If this is to be his final World Cup bow, as must surely be the case, it was a desperate way to say goodbye.

Argentina was pitiful for almost the entirety of this final. It did not manage a single shot until the 116th minute of the game — not just a shot on target, a single shot at all. Not one!

Lionel Messi slumped to the turf after Argentina's defeat. (Getty Images: Michael Regan)

It had clearly come with a plan to try to frustrate Spain, disrupt its rhythm with fouls and time-wasting, and hope Messi could create some magic the other way.

It succeeded on the first two fronts, but even Messi, playing a lone hand against a disciplined and tactically supreme Spain side, could not find a way to threaten.

Spain, to its credit, remained patient with the ball and alert to any danger in defence. It continued to play its football, metronomic at times and very easy on the eye at others, and eventually got the result it deserved.

As did Argentina.

5. Trump crashes the Spanish party

It had long since been telegraphed that Donald Trump's first game day appearance at this World Cup would be at the final, and that he would join his mate Gianni Infantino on the pitch to present the medals and trophy after the game.

And yet it remained a curious sight as he stood in the middle of the Spanish celebrations, aimlessly wandering around and gesturing towards players, refusing to get out of the way so they could enjoy the fulfilment of their lifelong dream.

Donald Trump inserted himself in Spain's World Cup celebrations. (Getty Images: Ian MacNicol)

This too was no surprise, as Trump did the same thing last year when Chelsea won the Club World Cup. That time, the players were surprised as Trump stood in the middle of the trophy celebration, grinning widely.

This time, Spain captain Rodri made a point of trying to usher him out of the way. Infantino chased him across the stage, trying in vain to get him out of the way. He remained on the periphery, but crucially still within camera shot, when Rodri lifted the World Cup.

Perhaps a perfect way to sum up this tournament. Brilliant at its heart, when the football was the focus, but rather unseemly in the background and on the fringes.

title: A quick breakdown of the offside rule
text:
Spain has just won the FIFA World Cup 1-0 in a match against Argentina that ran down to the wire.

During extra time, it looked as though Ferran Torres had scored a goal that would have brought Spain's lead to two and sealed a victory.

It was the second goal disallowed for Spain, this time for offside.

So what is the offside rule?

It's an infamous question asked by millions of football newbies every tournament.

If you've jumped on the World Cup bandwagon and found yourself trying to wrap your head around this section of the rule book, we're here to help.

Here's a quick breakdown of the rule.

Why does it exist?

The offside rule aims to prevent players from lingering next to the opponent's net to wait for a pass.

It forces teams to build up play carefully and use complex tactics to score goals.

Without the rule, teams would be incentivised to constantly launch long balls, making the game less dynamic.

So how does it work?

If a player is called for an offside offence, the referee will stop the play and award an indirect free kick to the opposing team.

The free kick is taken from the spot where the offside player had been located when their teammate passed them the ball.

What is a free kick? Read more A free kick is awarded when a player on one team does something wrong, with the referee giving the other team a chance to kick the ball while play has stopped. A penalty is essentially the same thing, except the kick is taken inside the penalty box, which is directly in front of the goal, and opposing players aren't allowed to interfere until after the shot is taken. Goals can be scored from either a free kick or a penalty kick, but they are more easily achieved from penalties.

When is a player offside?

A player is in an offside position if:

they're standing in their opponent's half; and

they're nearer to the goal than both the ball and the last line of defenders at the exact moment the ball is passed to them by a teammate.

However, an offside player is only penalised if they become involved in play.

And there's no offside offence if a player receives the ball directly from an opposition player, a goal kick, a throw-in or a corner kick.

title: 'Right fit': Ken Hinkley announced as inaugural Tasmania Devils coach
text:
Former Port Adelaide coach Ken Hinkley has been formally announced as the inaugural coach of the Tasmania Devils.

It completes a year-long hunt for a senior coach for the AFL's newest club, which included strong interest from high-profile candidates John Longmire and Nathan Buckley.

Hinkley will join the club on a five-year deal, which will take him through until the end of the 2031 season.

The appointment of Ken Hinkley as coach is the most consequential in the Devils' short history. (ABC News: Mitchell Woolnough)

"To all the Tassie believers, I just want to say thank you for the vote of confidence," Hinkley said in an initial video message, sent out to Devils supporters by the club.

"It's going to be another big day today when we join the journey together, and we look forward to where we're going to go from here.

" Such an exciting time for Tasmania and all the true believers. "

In a press release, Devils chair Grant O'Brien said he was "thrilled" to announce Ken Hinkley as the club's inaugural head coach.

"We didn't set out to find the candidate with the single best win-loss record in this process, we set out to find the right coach for exactly what Tasmania needs right now," he said.

"Ken's ability to build relationships with young players, and to build an environment that people want to be part of is exceptional, it is the number one reason we believe he is the right fit for this club."

'We're a football state,' Hinkley says

Hinkley appeared alongside Devils executives at a press conference later on Monday morning.

"When I get an opportunity to coach, young people particularly, it's something that I thrive in and I love, and I always have," he said.

"To coach the Tasmania Football Club, it's a special time.

" It's 160-plus years of football down here and as much as we're a startup club, we're a football state. "

He said he was particularly proud to be the team's inaugural coach.

Hinkley will be tasked with galvanising the state's three regions behind the new team. (ABC News: Mitchell Woolnough)

"In 100 years' time, when Tasmania have won so many premierships, at some point there will be a revision moment where we look back and go, 'Who was the first coach of the footy club?', and my name's going to be there forever," he said.

Hinkley said he was confident the team would attract plenty of supporters.

"All these great people down here have had teams they follow in the AFL, you watch the instant traction come to the Devils, and they will switch," he said.

Devils CEO Brendon Gale said Hinkley understood the challenges of leading a football team.

"He inherited Port Adelaide at a real low ebb in their future, both on and off field, and was involved in a substantial rebuild and reset," he said.

"He's universally regarded as a real relationship coach, perhaps regarded as the finest in the AFL.

"We feel he's a highly relatable, authentic personality which will represent Tasmania and the local football ecosystem … but also he'll represent our football club extremely well on a national stage."

Gale said Hinkley's five-year contract begins on November 1 this year.

Hinkley said his media commitments with Fox and SEN would continue in the meantime.

Longmire, Buckley unsuccessful

The 59-year-old Hinkley was whisked into Hobart yesterday morning for a final presentation to the Devils' coaching selection panel, which included O'Brien, CEO Brendon Gale, football director Alastair Lynch and culture boss Lauren Jauncey.

Devils coaching choice both a blessing and a curse The Tasmania Devils need to pick a coach to get them ready for their AFL entry.

It was Jauncey who accompanied Hinkley into Hobart yesterday.

Following a presentation to the panel at Hobart's Tasman Hotel yesterday afternoon, the decision to sign Hinkley was ratified by the board.

The appointment is the most consequential in the Devils' short history, surpassing that of inaugural CEO Brendon Gale in 2024, with Hinkley now charged with building the Devils into an on-field force and acting as the club's public face and spokesperson.

Tasmania had made no secret of securing a "people person" as its inaugural coach, with the JackJumpers NBL club's appointment of now championship-winning coach Scott Roth serving as inspiration.

Tasmania Devils chief executive Brendon Gale was part of the Devils' coaching selection panel. (ABC News: Mackenzie Heard)

On Friday, Gale spoke of the successful candidate having a "real intrinsic motivation" to coach the club.

"You've got to really want this job and be prepared to embrace all the challenges and opportunities that come with it," he said.

One of those challenges will be uniting Tasmania behind the Devils, whose popularity is strong but has been tarnished by at-times vicious debate over the construction of the estimated $1.13 billion Macquarie Point Stadium in Hobart.

Hinkley will also be confronted by the task of galvanising the state's three regions behind the team, which is seen by some as Hobart-centric.

Former Port Adelaide coach Ken Hinkley coached the team across 12 seasons, but has never coached a team to the finals. (ABC News: Ben Pettitt)

His coaching record will also be brought into sharper focus in the coming days, as the decision is analysed by the football public.

Across 12 seasons at Port Adelaide, Hinkley coached 282 home and away matches, winning 116 and losing 114 at a record of 59.6 per cent.

But in finals, that winning percentage dipped to just 40 per cent, with six wins coming from 15 appearances.

Hinkley has never coached a team to a grand final.

Fellow candidates John Longmire and Nathan Buckley — who had signalled his strong desire to coach the team more than 12 months ago — were informed on Sunday that they had been unsuccessful in their pursuit of the role.

title: Five-year wait for much-needed housing in WA's holiday hotspot
text:
A housing charity trying to build prefabricated, small homes for the homeless in one of Western Australia's premier tourist towns says red tape has proven a significant obstacle.

The development application for the long-vacant railway barracks area has finally been submitted after years of negotiations with the state government over use of the land.

The project was initially proposed by the local Rotary club five years ago as a way to provide social housing on disused land for an increasing number of homeless people across the region.

Julian Sanderson and Gordon Temby were among those who first proposed building tiny houses at the old railway barracks site. (ABC South West WA: Anthony Pancia)

MyHome founder Michelle Blakeley said the ongoing process to secure the peppercorn lease of the crown land from the state government had been lengthy, despite broader community support for more social and alternative housing.

"I think everyone from the shire president down would love to see more social housing in Margaret River, right through the South West and Great Southern," she said.

Michelle Blakeley says the process of providing small social housing can be lengthy. (Supplied: Michelle Blakeley)

"Because there's a high demand for holiday rentals, the [rents] in that area are very high, and a lot of people living in that area, even if they are employed, even as key workers, they just can't afford the rents in the area, because they are paying a premium."

Ms Blakeley said the charity had already delivered 30 small social homes on leased vacant land in North Fremantle and Albany, and 24 homes in Carlisle.

Margaret River's old railway barracks date back to the 1920s. (ABC South West WA: Anthony Pancia)

She said the process of obtaining land leases, checking, and securing government funding needed to be streamlined to ensure housing charities could help the homeless.

"We really do have to jump through hoops in a very elongated red tape procedure," she said.

" So that side of it is very frustrating, particularly when we get to winter. "

Small homes built by charity MyHome on leased land in North Fremantle.

She said the Department of Planning indicated negotiations would have to take place with local Aboriginal corporation Karri Karrak.

Karri Karrak chief executive Abby Phillis said while the land was initially intended to form part of the Noongar Land Estate, the corporation was broadly supportive of social and affordable housing being provided on the site.

Shire of Augusta Margaret River chief executive Andrea Sevley said the project would now be assessed.

"The shire has a long history of supporting initiatives that increase access to safe, secure, and affordable housing," she said.

The Margaret River proposal would be similar to these small homes built in North Fremantle. (Supplied: MyHome )

Process not to blame: government

A state government spokesperson rejected suggestions its processes were behind the project's slow progress, saying MyHome had only re-engaged with planning officials this year.

"MyHome advised that their own due diligence investigations concluded the site was not viable for a residential development," the spokesperson said.

"The land is eligible for inclusion in the Noongar Land Estate; however, it has not been offered to Karri Karrak Aboriginal Corporation.

"Its eligibility has not caused a delay."

Should the project be approved, the spokesman said DPLH would consider appropriate tenure to facilitate the development.

Tiny homes can struggle to find a space

Ms Blakeley stressed the project could best be described as small social homes, as separate from mobile "tiny homes", which are placed on a trailer and are privately owned.

Both have been touted as ways to ease the housing crisis, but getting necessary approvals can prove frustrating.

Earlier this year, the Shire of Capel gave a privately owned permanent tiny-homes-on-wheels community the green light. It is believed to be the first of its kind in Western Australia.

In New South Wales, Shellharbour Council has approved a trial of new planning rules to allow tiny homes on residential lots without a development application.

Modular homes on wheels have proven increasingly popular across Australia. (Supplied: Strive Homes )

NSW tiny home builder Brenden Morrissey said people who had purchased tiny homes sometimes struggled with legislative requirements governing where they can be placed.

"It's always a challenge," he said.

"We've got one lady that her tiny home is here, ready, and she's living in South Australia and is still looking for her spot."

Social housing provider Accordwest maintains a mix of housing types is needed to solve housing issues across Regional WA.

Chief executive Danelle Milward said there was no singular solution.

"We need a mix of social housing, affordable housing, crisis accommodation and innovative approaches, including modular housing and other models that can be delivered more quickly," she said.

title: Fears massive US private lending market sinking
text:
The muddy world of private lending has Australia's corporate regulator on high alert.

Wall Street is ground zero for the alternative investment market and there are fears this massive, risky US private lending ship is sinking and investors are jumping out.

And there are unique risks for Australia too.

"If the Australian property is overvalued, and we see those practices emerging and it happens at scale, you get gaps," ASIC commissioner Simone Constant told The Business.

"And when you get those gaps, you get problems with liquidity, you get lagging in data, you get the risk of default, for example.

"And of course, investors putting their money into things that don't stack up."

Simone Constant is concerned Australian investors could lose money if there is a private credit financial shock. (ABC News: John Gunn)

US private credit firm Blue Owl had to limit the amount of money investors could withdraw this year as the company bled value from souring software investments.

The firm's two private credit funds again faced elevated redemptions — investors seeking to withdraw cash — in the June quarter.

Its shares have plummeted 40 per cent this year as investors have fretted about the lender's exposure to ailing software companies.

US auto lender Tricolor Holdings and UK mortgage lender Market Financial Solutions have already collapsed.

Central banks and regulators watching closely

It has caught the attention of global central banks.

In December, the Bank of England launched a system-wide exploratory scenario exercise to enhance its understanding of broader risks and dynamics in private markets, according to Reserve Bank Freedom of Information (FOI) documents.

Bank of England governor Andrew Bailey told Bloomberg in May: "There are some signs of strain in the market."

The Bank of England, led by Andrew Bailey, wants to better understand the risks and dynamics in private markets. (AP: Toby Melville/Pool)

"We are seeing some increase in requests," he said.

"Quite a big increase in requests for outflows, to take liquidity out."

The results of the UK central bank's review are expected to be published in early 2027.

Documents obtained under FOI, originally requested by Nine, reveal the Reserve Bank has also been making inquiries into financial and economic risks for Australia related to the opaque private credit sector.

ASIC's November 2025 private credit surveillance report noted the "market is growing rapidly, but there are some areas where improvement is needed — and in some cases, materially so".

Wave of money moves out of software

Private credit refers to lending outside the banking sector.

Software companies received much of this non-bank funding earlier this decade, but the flow of funds has since moved towards AI.

Verdad Adviser managing partner Dan Rasmussen has been warning of major global financial stability risks from an eventual implosion of US private credit.

Loading...

"We're going to see more and more pain," he warned.

Mr Rasmussen is worried about a negative feedback loop in which software companies default on their debt and lead to further panic in private credit markets.

"Now what's happening is that there's $100 billion of loans outstanding and there's only $50 billion of new fundraising and private credit," he said.

"Well, that's going to force defaults on itself because there is no-one else who's willing to lend to these companies.

"No bank is going to lend to them, so either the private credit lends to them or they go bankrupt."

Global credit crunch risk

Australia's corporate regulator is bracing for the rising risk of another global credit crunch.

"Private credit is now at a size and at a breadth that hasn't been seen before and certainly hasn't been tested in a downturn," ASIC's Simone Constant said.

"So, there would be bumps."

Locally, there is currently $250 billion worth of private credit loans, up from roughly $35 billion a decade ago.

Nick Kelly thinks the Austrailan market will face challenges in the future. (ABC News: John Gunn)

"It's not just been the retail investors in the market. The institutional investors, superannuation funds and others have piled into this asset class," Wilson Asset Management portfolio manager Nick Kelly said.

"I think the Australian market will face its own challenges in the future, just given the amount of capital that's being deployed into this market, into assets that I don't think are as safe as some people perceive them to be."

And Australia's $4.5 trillion superannuation sector is high on the regulator's worry list.

"So increasingly, every working Australian, investing Australian does have exposure to private credit," Ms Constant said.

Over half of all private lending in Australia is concentrated in property development and construction.

ASIC says it is monitoring loans in this space, but it does not have as much information as it would like.

Australian financial shock

ASIC is concerned a property market crash could trigger a private credit financial shock.

Australian private credit provider Brett Craig, who is Aura Group's director of private credit, said investors in Australian property finance needed to be careful.

"Lending against property construction transactions is a good way to make money if you know what you're doing and you can actually step in should the borrower default," he said.

Brett Craig warns investors in Australian property finance need to be careful. (ABC News: John Gunn)

"But it is a very good way to lose money if you don't have that ability to step in and complete a project as an example."

The concern for regulators, ultimately, is that private investors and superannuants end up footing the bill for weak investments.

"We want there to be confidence in private credit," Ms Constant said.

"What could go wrong? Investors could lose money.

"Investors [may not] understand they're going to lose money, and that could happen at some scale."

Dan Rasmussen said every Australian should know if they were exposed to private credit.

"What we don't know yet is who owns it, to what extent [big US companies own it], and what the downstream consequences are when they find out how bad it's going to be, right?" Mr Rasmussen said.

"And the biggest risk I'd be worried [about] if I was in Australia is how much the superannuation schemes own of it.

"How exposed are they?

"And what happens if this stuff turns out to be materially worse than it looks now?"

title: Boxing community mourns death of rising US star Hannah Rapp
text:
American boxer Hannah Rapp has been killed in a collision with a car while she was cycling in Brazos, Texas.

The 26-year-old rising boxing star had won eight and drawn one of her first nine professional bouts, capturing the North American Boxing Federation featherweight title.

Last month she challenged Tiara Brown for the World Boxing Council (WBC) featherweight championship, losing by unanimous decision.

Ms Brown paid tribute to Rapp online saying she was "the best dance partner I have ever faced as a pro".

WBC president Mauricio Sulaiman also shared a statement in the wake of the tragedy, describing Rapp as "an exceptional boxer, but above all, an invaluable member of our boxing family".

"We join in the grief that overwhelms her loved ones, her team, and all those who had the privilege of knowing and supporting her throughout her brilliant career," he said.

Internet personality and founder of boxing and mixed martial arts promotion company Most Valuable Promotions, Jake Paul, called Rapp a "warrior" in a tribute posted on X.

"Gone too soon. Condolences to her entire clan," he said.

The Brazos County sheriff's office said it had identified the driver of the vehicle as 31-year-old Charles Medina, who has been charged with manslaughter.

Reuters/ABC

Advertisement

Wingman Xavier Duursma (hamstring) faces a spell on the sidelines. The Bombers were able to sing the club song for only the second time in 31 games after edging the shocked Giants. It was their first win since beating Melbourne in Gather Round in Adelaide on April 11, while they had not tasted victory in Victoria since May last year. It was Dean Solomon’s first win as caretaker coach since replacing Brad Scott in May. While only 14,601 fans attended the game, the scenes of joy for the Bombers were palpable. “It’s a bloody good feeling. Our footy club’s been on its knees for a period of time, and the footy world’s jumped on that, and they enjoy us sitting at the bottom,” Solomon said.

Advertisement

“We’ve felt that, and we don’t like that, so to push back a little bit and get one win, albeit one win, I’m just really happy for these players.” Keep up to date with the best AFL coverage in the country. Sign up for the Real Footy newsletter.

title: Olympio clinches high-grade Idaho silver project with scale
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears Olympio clinches high-grade Idaho silver project with scale Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Penny Taylor July 20, 2026 — 1:41pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Olympio Metals has secured an 80 per cent interest in the Jacknife high-grade silver project in northern Idaho, USA, in a strategic move that further expands its North American precious and critical minerals portfolio. Rather than starting with a grassroots exploration project, Olympio is acquiring an advanced silver district with substantial underground development and a proven mining history. Entrance to the Conjecture Mine at Olympio Metals’ Jacknife silver project in Idaho’s Lakeview Mining District, USA. The centrepiece of the deal is the Conjecture mine, with a historical foreign estimate of 632,081 tonnes grading 405 grams per tonne (g/t) for 8.22 million ounces of contained silver. The company says the estimate predates the modern JORC Code and will require validation drilling before it can be formally assessed as a mineral resource under the code. The project already boasts a proven production history, with six mines producing more than 3.2 million ounces of silver at an average grade of 395g/t. The Conjecture mine alone produced more than two million ounces between 1956 and 1964 at grades of up to 684g/t silver.

Advertisement

‘The project has historically produced over 3.2Moz of silver grading 12.7 ounces per tonne.’ Olympio Metals managing director Sean Delaney Previous drilling also confirmed high-grade silver mineralisation beyond the underground workings, including 3.9m grading 264g/t silver and 1.88g/t gold, supporting Olympio’s planned validation drilling. While historic mining appears to confirm the grade, more than 8km of underground development, including a shaft extending beyond 600m at Conjecture, gives Olympio a significant head start in proving the project’s scale. Those workings extend across only 500m of the 3.4km-long Conjecture Shear, leaving much of the mineralised system largely untested and providing significant exploration upside across multiple structural targets. Olympio Metals managing director Sean Delaney said: “The Jacknife project in Idaho is an excellent acquisition for Olympio to complement our recently acquired Raven high grade silver project. Jacknife has historically produced over 3.2 million ounces of silver at an average grade of 12.7 ounces per tonne over a period of very low silver prices. The Conjecture Mine within the project area hosts a significant foreign estimate of 8.22Moz at 13 oz/t silver and remains open along strike and at depth.”

Advertisement

Olympio’s immediate focus is a maiden drill program to validate the historical foreign estimate by testing known mineralisation within the Conjecture Shear zone between existing underground levels. About 80 per cent of the project’s seven-kilometre mineralised trend sits on patented mining claims, allowing exploration without federal permits and requiring only limited approvals for underground mining. Thanks to the patents, drilling is expected to begin as soon as a suitable rig becomes available. Olympio is acquiring the project from Idaho-based Scout Discoveries, which consolidated the patented and surrounding claims. Scout will retain a 20 per cent interest while providing geological, exploration, drilling and operational support as Olympio’s local technical partner. Under the terms of the acquisition, the company has stitched together a low-cost pathway into the project, paying just US$20,000 (A$29,000) upfront for an exclusive 30-day due diligence period, followed by US$100,000 (A$143,000) in cash and 45 million Olympio shares on completion to secure its 80 per cent interest. The deal is also heavily weighted towards success, with most of the value only payable if the project advances. Scout Discoveries can earn a further 25 million shares if Olympio starts drilling, secures key drilling approvals, and defines a JORC-compliant resource of at least 3 million silver-equivalent ounces within five years.

Advertisement

Beyond exploration, additional cash payments only kick in as the project is de-risked through development, with US$500,000 (A$715,000) due on completion of a preliminary economic assessment. A further US$1 million (A$1.43M) is payable on a pre-feasibility study and an additional US$1 million on completion of a definitive feasibility study. Beyond validating the foreign estimate, the company has already identified multiple drill-ready targets and will complement drilling with surface mapping and geochemical sampling to prioritise further opportunities. Olympio has also secured a right of first refusal over the historic Lakeview Mill, less than 2km from Conjecture, providing strategic future processing optionality. The acquisition follows Olympio’s recent purchase of the nearby Raven high-grade silver project in neighbouring Montana and the Sawtooth antimony project in Idaho. Together, the duo of acquisitions complement the company’s strategy of assembling advanced precious and critical minerals assets across established North American mining districts, including its flagship Bousquet gold project in Quebec. Jacknife lies within Idaho’s Lakeview Mining District, 35km from the town of Coeur d’Alene. The grounds are hosted in the same geological formations as the Sunshine Mine in the world-famous Silver Valley, a world-class system with a reported silver endowment of 624 million ounces.

Advertisement

The US Government’s US$12 billion strategic mineral stockpile initiative highlights growing demand for domestic supplies of critical minerals. Jacknife also contains significant lead, zinc, copper, gold and minor antimony alongside silver, all benefiting from the growing demand in solar, electronics and defence. Jacknife delivers Olympio far more than another exploration project. It provides an advanced platform to quickly unlock value in one of North America’s premier silver districts. And a proven production history, an existing historical foreign estimate and substantial exploration upside appear to have combined to create a powerful investment proposition. title: Brisbane all-boys school to undergo culture review after harassment claims
text:
Advertisement

NationalQueenslandPrivate schools Brisbane all-boys school to undergo culture review after harassment claims Catherine Strohfeldt July 20, 2026 — 12:40pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Marist College Ashgrove will undergo an external review, following allegations of harassment and a “culture of misogyny” at the Brisbane all-boys Catholic school, including claims a female teacher was surrounded by hundreds of students who hurled food at her. Parents were notified on Monday morning in a letter from acting head of college Paul Blinkoff, who assumed the position after head of college Michael Newman took leave for the rest of the school year. Blinkoff said the review would respond to “a number of issues that have been raised”, focusing specifically on workplace culture. The independent review would follow public accounts from Marist College Ashgrove teachers and staff, tendered to court within a claim for damages filed in July last year. Marist College Ashgrove “The final details of this review are being determined, however, it will have at its centre staff, and a commitment to listen, hear and understand the issues, ideas and thoughts they want to share about our workplace culture,” he said.

Advertisement

He pledged to share the findings of the review with staff, families and the wider community. “It is important for Marist College Ashgrove and Marist Schools Australia that we do not in any way pre-empt the review or what it may find,” Blinkoff said. The independent review would follow public accounts from college teachers and staff, tendered to the Queensland Supreme Court in late April within a claim for damages filed in July last year. Drama teacher Victoria Sparrow alleged she was surrounded and pelted with food by hundreds of boys on October 27, 2023, while she was employed at the school.

Advertisement

Sparrow’s legal representative, Gerard Forde, told the court the accounts supported claims the school had a “history of misbehaviour towards women”, and fostered a “culture of misogyny” in its all-male student body. He said Sparrow was supervising students at lunchtime on a rainy day in 2023, with the boys restricted to an outdoor covered area before they turned on her. Related Article Private schools Call for external review into harassment at Brisbane boys school “[Sparrow] has said there had been a deterioration of the behaviour of the children, and it had occurred over a long period of time,” Forde said. “These are examples of mistreatment of women leading up to what occurred, and so the school would have been aware of it.”

Advertisement

In late June, Independent Education Union Queensland branch secretary Terry Burke urged the school to commission an independent review into a “broader culture of harassment and sexualised behaviour”. Burke said the union had been contacted about the “specific incident” in 2023, but was concerned about ongoing reports of “objectionable behaviour and harassment”. “Serious issues persist at the school,” Burke said. On Monday, the acting head of college said the review would be external, and provide “various” opportunities for staff to contribute. The letter also announced the appointment of a new deputy head of school as of Monday, Danielle Greig.
