title: True North cashes in on non-core project to fuel Qld copper growth
text:
Advertisement

Sponsored BusinessCompaniesBulls N' Bears True North cashes in on non-core project to fuel Qld copper growth Brought to you by BULLS N’ BEARS Murray Ward July 20, 2026 — 12:36pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

True North Copper has executed a savvy piece of business, divesting its non-core Bundarra copper project in a deal that brings in cash while retaining exposure to future success, allowing the company to pour resources into its two flagship copper projects in Northwest Queensland. The company has entered a conditional agreement to sell Bundarra to private explorer Tec Minerals for $1.5 million, comprising $500,000 in cash and $1M equity in Tec post-listing. The deal also includes a one per cent net smelter royalty. The Wallace North prospect, pictured, within True North Copper’s broader Cloncurry copper project. The cash consideration is split between a $50,000 non-refundable deposit, already received and $450,000 payable on completion. Tec also retains the right to repurchase the royalty from True North for $3 million. Completing the deal is conditional on Tec successfully undertaking an IPO or being acquired through a reverse takeover and on all regulatory approvals being received. The deal is slated to be finalised by late 2026 or early 2027.

Advertisement

‘The transaction generates immediate value while retaining long-term upside through the royalty.’ True North Copper managing director Andrew Mooney The sale allows True North to recycle capital and sharpen focus on its core growth assets at the Mt Oxide and Cloncurry copper projects, where the drill rods are already turning. By retaining an equity stake and a royalty, management says it aims to keep a foot on the Bundarra accelerator without having to fund the exploration drive itself. True North Copper managing director Andrew Mooney said: “The sale of Bundarra is consistent with our strategy of simplifying the portfolio and focusing capital and management on our core copper assets in Northwest Queensland. The transaction generates immediate value while retaining long-term upside through the royalty.” True North’s priority at Mt Oxide lies at the company’s recently discovered Aquila deposit, which sits 10 kilometres along strike from its impressive 15.03 million tonne Vero resource grading 1.46 per cent copper and 10.59 grams per tonne (g/t) silver.

Advertisement

A new round of drilling has kicked off at Aquila, following recent geophysical work that almost doubled the target’s interpreted strike from 1km to 1.8km. The drill program is designed to confirm the geophysical extensions and get a better handle on the geometry and controls of the mineralisation, with assays expected in late July. True North says the geophysical markers have lined up neatly with last year’s high-grade drill results, adding a layer of confidence to the emerging system. Previous drilling at Aquila certainly gave the company something to write home about, with broad, shallow hits including a 145-metre intercept grading 0.75 per cent copper, 0.12 per cent cobalt and 2.9g/t silver. That hit also contained a richer 53-metre zone going 1.18 per cent copper. Another hole returned a hefty 7m running at 7.9 per cent copper within a broader 59-metre section at 1.77 per cent copper.

Advertisement

The latest exploration push comes hot on the heels of a busy period for the company. Two weeks ago, True North tabled a new high-grade copper-gold structure at Wallace North, which forms part of its Cloncurry copper project. Diamond drilling there returned a 24.5-metre intercept at 2.17 per cent copper and 2.01g/t gold for a copper equivalent grade of 4.21 per cent. The interval also included a stunning 3.3-metre section grading a bonanza 10.11 per cent copper and 7.93g/t gold. The company says when the results come to hand, they will be fed into the ongoing prefeasibility study and ore reserve for the Cloncurry project, which is due for delivery in the December quarter of this year. That study is also assessing the integration of the recently acquired Mongoose deposit with the company’s existing and adjoining Taipan open pit. The study will also investigate growth options for Wallace North and the gold-copper potential of its 56,000-ounce Wynberg gold deposit. By selling off a non-core asset to funnel funds into its two key projects at Cloncurry and Mt Oxide, both of which are delivering significant exploration news, True North appears to be tidying up its portfolio at precisely the right time. title: Trump’s company is failing. This is his shady plan to give it a lifeline
text:
Advertisement

BusinessMarketsWall Street Opinion Trump’s company is failing. This is his shady plan to give it a lifeline Stephen Bartholomeusz Senior business columnist July 20, 2026 — 11:59am

July 20, 2026 — 11:59am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The Trump family has shown a remarkable ability to monetise Donald Trump’s second term in the White House. Their latest effort is turning his social media posts into a financial asset. Last week, the Trump Media & Technology Group announced a plan to charge investors $US100,000 ($143,000) a month for instant access to Trump’s posts on his Truth Social platform. Some investors have reportedly already signed up. During the war in the Middle East, Trump’s posts have significantly moved markets. Bloomberg Trump Media has launched a real-time data feed via APIs (application programming interfaces) that would give subscribers earlier access to Trump’s posts than non-subscribers. Given that Trump’s posts often move markets, that advantage – measured in milliseconds – could be hugely profitable for hedge funds, Wall Street banks, algorithmic traders and wealthy private investors. Institutional traders couldn’t afford not to pay up to get that access.

Advertisement

Related Article Opinion

Trump's America ‘Trump Airport’ could be a new cash cow for the president’s empire Trump Media didn’t explicitly say that subscribers to the service would get privileged access to Trump’s posts – it said they would get access to the 10 highest-ranking accounts – but Trump, with 12.9 million followers, is the highest-ranked account, with his sons, Donald Junior and Eric, also among the most popular. In its pitch documents to prospective subscribers, the company also reportedly highlighted 10 Trump posts that moved markets. No one would pay the $US100,000 a month (or $US60,000 a month if they sign up for three years) for anything other than Trump’s posts. Trump’s second term as president has been an immensely successful one for him and his family in adding to their wealth, with most calculations of the wealth added in his first year back in office topping $US2 billion – some estimates are far higher – thanks largely to the success of the family’s crypto businesses.

Advertisement

While those dealings, and the family’s fortuitously well-timed investments in companies just before they received hefty government contracts, have generated controversy, the proposed monetisation of Trump’s social media posts by a company that he has a 41 per cent shareholding in creates a clear and obvious conflict of interest and breach of ethical norms. Trump’s posts are often announcements – the first announcements – of US government policy. Effectively, the Trump Media plan is to charge for early access to those announcements so that wealthy insiders can profit. Ever since its launch in 2021, Trump Media has been a vehicle in search of a profitable business. Bloomberg Other social media companies, like X, also charge for API access – but they don’t have the presidential posts. That prior access to the posts, even if for milliseconds, can be valuable is irrefutable.

Advertisement

During the war in the Middle East, Trump’s posts have significantly moved markets, whether it’s the market for oil, shares or even bonds. Related Article Donald Trump Trump has revealed how much money he made last year, and it’s unprecedented When he used Truth Social to announce he was imposing 25 per cent tariffs on Mexico and Canada in February, the peso and Canadian dollar fell significantly. When he announced a US cryptocurrency reserve, Bitcoin’s value jumped more than 10 per cent. In April, having posted that it was a “great time to buy,” he announced his Liberation Day tariffs would be paused for 90 days and the sharemarket soared.

Advertisement

His touting of particular stocks has led to significant price movements. There is, therefore, plenty of evidence that his posts move markets. Institutional traders couldn’t afford to not pay up to get early access to Trump’s Truth Social posts. Truth Social It’s not clear whether the Trump Media plan is legal or not and, in any event, thanks to the US Supreme Court, Trump has immunity for his actions as president. It is also unlikely that the US Securities and Exchange Commission or any other government agency would, in this administration, even consider the question. The Democrats in Congress are, as you’d expect, outraged, but their Republican colleagues have shown no interest in pursuing Trump’s profiteering.

Advertisement

The midterms could, of course, change the balance of Congressional power, but whether they could do anything to stop Trump Media from profiting from presidential posts is doubtful. US presidents and vice presidents are not covered by the conflict of interest and self-enrichment provisions that apply to other government officials. Trump also has the presidential pardon that he could deploy, if required, to protect his family and other Trump Media officials. Trump Media would, in any case, argue that it isn’t selectively offering privileged access to Trump’s posts. Trump, with 12.9 million followers, is the highest-ranked account, with his sons, Donald Junior and Eric, also among the most popular. Bloomberg It publishes the posts and makes them available to all Truth Social subscribers at the same time – those paying for API access are arbitraging latency – the time it takes for traffic from the site to reach normal users – rather than being provided access to the posts ahead of their publication.

Advertisement

Latency is why institutions and traders co-locate their trading technology with securities exchanges. Milliseconds matter. Even without APIs, some traders have made a lot of money from front-running Trump posts, which has given rise to suspicion of insider information. US presidents and vice presidents are not covered by the conflict of interest and self-enrichment provisions that apply to other government officials. One of Donald Trump’s teleprompter operators has been suspended after allegedly making bets on prediction markets about what the president would say in his speeches. There have been some very big and successful wagers made on those markets – and in share and futures markets – ahead of Trump’s Truth Social posts, including the capture of Venezuelan president, Nicolás Maduro, the killing of Iran’s Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and other developments in the conflict with Iran, along with prescient bets on the US economic data that Trump has sometimes released, ahead of the official release, on his platform.

Advertisement

Ever since its launch in 2021 (after Trump was de-platformed by other sites after the January 6 violence on Capitol Hill), Trump Media has been a vehicle in search of a profitable business. Related Article Billionaires The controversial deal feeding the Trump family’s biggest money-maker As a social media platform, it has been a dud, with a sliver of the audience of competing platforms. Its original vision of becoming a massive digital media conglomerate, with video-on-demand, podcasts and news services, hasn’t come close to being realised. It has diversified into cryptocurrencies and financial services, expressed interest in establishing a predictions market for sports betting and is currently merging with a leading fusion energy researcher. Plans to spin out Truth Social ahead of that merger appear to have been abandoned. Trump Media has lost about 90 per cent of the sharemarket value it had when it launched and has lost money ever since that launch, with losses of more than $US2 billion in the past two years. In the first quarter of this year, it reported another $US405.9 million loss – on revenue of less than $US900,000 – albeit that most of those losses were non-cash.

Advertisement

The plan to allow Trump Media to profit from the presidency offers a lifeline for the company. The monetisation of Trump’s truths might also allow the proposed spin-off of Truth Social to be revisited because every institutional investor and algorithmic trader would need to sign up to the service, which could generate hundreds of millions of dollars a year – even billions – for the beleaguered company. title: Moon landing: Why this Chinese AI model rattled global sharemarkets
text:
Advertisement

TechnologyAI Moon landing: Why this Chinese AI model rattled global sharemarkets Tim Biggs July 20, 2026 — 11:50am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

China’s Moonshot AI, co-founded by former Google and Meta researcher Yang Zhilin, rattled western markets last week with the release of an impressive new model called Kimi K3. Now, the company reportedly plans to list publicly with a valuation of more than $43 billion. The company says its open-weight AI model outperforms all rivals with the exception of the most powerful; Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI’s GPT-5.6. Demand for the massive 2.8 trillion parameter model was so huge over the weekend that Moonshot had to temporarily pause sign-ups. Users have enthused about its coding and natural language capabilities, and compared its long context and efficiency favourable to the most popular US-developed models. Moonshot co-founder Yang Zhilin has a PhD and previously worked at Google, Meta and Huawei. Getty On release last week, a global sell-off affecting chip-makers and big tech wiped out trillions in market capitalisation. Bloomberg and others are now reporting that Moonshot has advised investors it hit annual recurring revenue of $US300 million ($430 million) in June, and is wrapping up a funding round that may value it at more than $US30 billion ($43 billion), ahead of a listing in as early as six months. Though still in his early thirties, Zhilin has a PhD in computer science from Carnegie Mellon University, is a serial startup founder and has worked at Google, Meta and Huawei. He co-founded Moonshot in 2023. It has largely lived in the shadow of other Chinese AI startups DeepSeek and Z.AI before now, though its long-memory chatbot Kimi began attracting hype earlier this year. With K3, the company has burst into mainstream relevance.

Advertisement

Related Article China relations Not a solo performance, but a symphony: What China’s leader thinks about AI The model is the best-performing open-weight technology yet to emerge from China. Open-weight refers to an AI model where the trained files are available for anyone to download. It’s not a fully open source, in the sense that users don’t necessarily have insight into how the model was trained. But open weight models (like Moonshot’s K3, and Meta’s Llama) differ significantly from the closed offerings of the big tech companies because anyone can run, host or customise them. OpenAI, Anthropic and Google only offer access to their models through paid APIs. The market reaction to Moonshot’s news mirrors the shockwaves we’ve seen previously whenever a Chinese lab makes a major AI breakthrough, with temporary sell-offs in Western semiconductor and AI tech stocks, as investors reconsider assumptions about how wide the US lead in AI technology actually is. However, Moonshot’s move is quite unlike the early DeepSeek releases that previously prompted a knee-jerk panic. While DeepSeek’s focus on ultra-low prices stoked fears of a democratisation and rapid commoditisation, Moonshot shows that the open-source gap has shrunk faster than expected. Developers now have access to frontier-class reasoning and coding capabilities, without needing to lock themselves into closed APIs. The company’s reported revenue shows that businesses are willing to pay to build on it.

Advertisement

Editor's pick Analysis

AI AI-powered Siri is on the way – but is it worth using? But that’s not to say Moonshot is cheaper or easier to run than any other mid-tier enterprise AI, including the big western ones. K3 is a massive model that requires serious hardware to host; you can’t simply download and run it on a regular PC. Consumer users will go through Moonshot’s Kimi chatbot to access the model, and that comes with a tiered subscription. Enterprises and developers will rent access to servers or custom installations, and the Chinese company’s API pricing is in line with competitors. Of course, licensing agreements stop another company installing K3 and on-selling access without giving Moonshot a cut. History suggests the market will settle as investors realise that a rising tide lifts all boats, and that increased competition and a multi-polar market for AI may ultimately be good for the likes of Nvidia, TSMC and even rival AI firms. But K3 has proven that open models can rival closed ones when used for high-value tasks, like coding and agents. title: First home buyer nabs Croydon house sold by investor for $1.83m
text:
Advertisement

PropertyNewsAuctions First home buyer nabs Croydon house sold by investor for $1.83m Carmen Forward July 20, 2026 — 11:50am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A charming two-bedroom home in Croydon was offloaded by an investor and snapped up by a first home buyer for $1,832,000 at auction on Saturday. The full brick single level home at 49 Wetherill Street came with dramatic fireplaces, an updated bathroom and a spacious backyard with covered outdoor area. The property was one of 564 scheduled auctions in Sydney last week. By Saturday evening, Domain Group recorded a preliminary auction clearance rate of 49 per cent from 325 reported results, while 114 auctions were withdrawn. Withdrawn auctions are counted as unsold properties when calculating the clearance rate. Bidding for the Croydon home opened at its $1.6 million guide, and went up in a flurry of varying bids between three buyers.

Advertisement

Bids climbed in mostly $10,000 and $20,000 increments before slowing to smaller rises of $1000 and $2000. Related Article Auctions Edwardian-era harbourside apartment passes in at $2.2 million At $1,831,000 the property was called three times before an additional $500 each was accepted from the final two bidders. It sold under the hammer for $1,832,000. Auctioneer Tom Panos said, “most of the properties I had today had no one registered or one person registered … But for some strange reason, this property ended up getting 10 people registered on it.” Panos said the reserve was around $1.75 million.

Advertisement

“It was a good result, and it did have 10 people. But … it probably would have got that price, or even a little bit more a few months ago,” he said. “There’s a lot of vendors that are feeling distraught because they’re having to sell their properties 10 to 15 per cent lower than what they would have maybe six months, a year ago. “But I keep reminding real estate agents and their vendors: let’s not forget, most of these houses have had a 60 per cent uplift since COVID, and that is probably one of the other reasons why we’re getting this adjustment. It was an unsustainable growth.” It was sold by Lambros Markessinis from Ray White Burwood.

Advertisement

The house last traded for $185,000, in 1993, and was rented for $670 per week, records show. A five-bedroom manor in Hornsby drew 11 registrations at auction. The grand residence at 34-36 Manor Road was guided at $2.25 million. Bidding opened at $2 million and went up sporadically in a mix of $25,000, $50,000 and $10,000 bids. Eleven parties registered and six were active before a crowd.

Advertisement

When bidding reached its $2.3 million reserve the charming home was announced as on the market. It sold for $2.69 million to a builder from the area. Related Article Auctions Young family gets a $50,000 discount on stylish $2.9m Greenwich house There is no legal requirement for a vendor’s reserve to be in line with their property’s price guide. Selling agent James Campbell from Belle Property Upper North Shore said, “It’s a unique home, and because it’s on such a big block of land … it was a rare finding. “So it just brought out lots of buyers … you would have never known the market’s down.”

Advertisement

The vendors are downsizing closer to the city. In Ashfield, a six-bedroom house in need of work at 95 Holden Street, passed in at auction. The “blank canvas” guided at $2.2 million had an opening offer of $1.9 million and a further increase to $1.95 million, however the vendor did not want to sell at that price. Selling agent Michael Simpson from Ray White Ashfield and Summer Hill said two parties registered, however one did not show.

Advertisement

The property last traded for $235,000 in 1991, records show. In Blakehurst, a five-bedroom contemporary property with a swimming pool sold for $3.36 million, which was $160,000 above its $3.2 million guide and reserve. The family home at 11 Lorna Avenue had five registered buyers, all local upsizing families. Bidding opened at $3 million and four parties competed in $20,000, $10,000, $5000 and $1000 rises. Selling agent Trent Tarbey from McGrath Sans Souci said, “It was relatively timid bidding. The bidding flowed consistently … Everyone was happy to bid and get involved, but no one was stretching themselves.”

Advertisement

The vendors are upsizing to Cronulla. The home last traded for $3.09 million in 2021, records show. AMP chief economist Dr Shane Oliver said Domain’s clearance rate of 49 per cent for Sydney is “a pretty soft result”. He noted the clearance rate was “lower in the pandemic and in the 2017 to 2019 property slump.” Considering the interest rate hikes, low levels of confidence, uncertainty in the economy, and investment property tax changes, Oliver said it’s to be expected that there is a slump in the property market. “It looks to me like a normal, a cyclical downturn,” he said.

text:
Advertisement

Updated SportNRLNRL 2026 Joey Manu to tour PNG Chiefs set-up as latest cross-code target Dan Walsh July 20, 2026 — 11:48am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Former Roosters star and rugby union convert Joey Manu has emerged as the PNG Chiefs’ latest marquee signing target as he tours the incoming franchise’s Port Moresby facilities. Manu was spotted at Sydney International Airport on Monday morning alongside PNG football manager Michael Chammas and head of recruitment Liam Ayoub as they travelled to the PNG capital as the club continues to build its inaugural 2028 roster. Loading Manu’s PNG sojourn is the first step in the Chiefs’ recruitment process, with the 29-year-old still signed for another year at French rugby union outfit Racing 92. Manu left rugby league and the Roosters at the height of his powers at the end of 2024 and has been regularly linked with a return to the 13-man code since.

Advertisement

He initially signed with Japanese rugby’s Toyota Verblitz before shifting to France, where he will play until June next season. PNG’s courting of Manu comes alongside continued negotiations with former NSW Origin winger and Wallabies hopeful Zac Lomax, who has been offered a multi-year deal by the Chiefs. Zac Lomax’s potential return to rugby league is gaining momentum. Getty Images Lomax is committed to seeing out the two years of his Western Force contract, having knocked back interest from Melbourne for him to return to the NRL next year, but would also come off-contract in time to feature in the Chiefs first campaign. Both he and Manu present as elite backline options for the Chiefs and offer versatility as well given Manu regularly filled in at fullback for the Roosters and New Zealand during his 181-game NRL career.

Advertisement

Joey Manu and PNG Chiefs football manager Michael Chammas at Sydney Airport on Monday. A full-time shift to the No.1 jumper was regularly discussed as a potential lure whenever Manu came off contract with the Roosters, but his ties to the Tricolours meant he never truly explored linking with an NRL rival. The Roosters were long considered Manu’s most likely destination were he to return to the NRL. However, this masthead reported in April that the club privately gave up that hope due to Manu’s earning power, with his Racing 92 salary estimated at around €1.3 million ($2 million). The Chiefs’ ability to offer tax-free wages, plus tax exemptions on any third-party agreement earnings spent in the Pacific nation, give the new franchise an ability to match overseas rugby markets that NRL rivals cannot. PNG’s pursuit of Manu and Lomax comes after their recruitment of Jarome Luai, Alex Johnston, Connor Watson and English prop Matty Lees as foundation players for 2028. The Chiefs will be able to table official offers to NRL players coming off contract from November.

Advertisement

There is no restriction on the new NRL franchise approaching potential recruits in rugby. More to come.

title: Olympic budget blowouts will be covered by private money, minister pledges
text:
Advertisement

NationalQueenslandRoad to Brisbane 2032 Olympic budget blowouts will be covered by private money, minister pledges Julius Dennis July 20, 2026 — 11:48am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Queensland’s Olympics minister says private money will pay for any potential funding blowouts for the 2032 Games, sticking to the government line no taxpayer money will be used to run the event. The pledge from Tim Mander – also the state minister of sport and racing – comes as the operational budget of the Olympic and Paralympic Games is being reviewed after organising committee boss Andrew Liveris said the $4.9 billion estimate bore “no resemblance to reality” earlier this year. The state and federal governments have committed $7.1 billion to building or upgrading 17 venues across Queensland, and the state has pencilled in a further $3.5 billion for four athletes villages. Tim Mander said delivering the first on-budget Olympics was the government’s aim. Matt Dennien Mander said the exact cost of the Games was “a little bit flexible” while the official list of sports and their venues were being nutted out, and that the governments would stick to that expenditure.

Advertisement

He said he hoped the sports were locked in by the International Olympic Committee by the end of the year. Related Article Government debt Post-COVID debt explosion a major challenge for all states, bar one “As things become more concrete, we’ll know the actual cost, and we can adjust accordingly,” he said. “Our aim is to make sure Queensland taxpayers aren’t left with the bill, and that’s one of my major objectives from an operational point of view.” When questioned on the “flexible” costs, Mander said the government would find ways to generate money.

Advertisement

“What I mean by that is it means if we’ve got to raise more money, if we’ve got to generate more ticket sales, we [will] look at other revenue-raising opportunities to meet whatever cost it might be,” he said. An artist’s impression of the proposed Brisbane Stadium, to be built at Victoria Park. The minister pointed to a Commonwealth Bank sponsorship already inked for the Olympics, which the Australian Financial Review reported to be worth $200 million, as an example of where money could come from, adding the government was on the hunt to lock in more deals. He said the government would not can regional events despite the potential savings of a more centralised event. “The state is quite electric in the regional towns about the events that are coming to them,” Mander said.

title: Third-degree burns and skin grafts: The popular medical device that left a trail of injured children
text:
Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

An investigation into medical devices reveals alarming flaws in both the machines we put in our bodies and the system that oversees them. See all stories .

We were in the trenches. My seven-month-old baby had croup and his little body rattled through the night as he barked like a seal. Like many desperate parents wanting a solution, I drove to the nearest pharmacy. In the third aisle, I thought I’d found the solution: a bulky, plastic humidifier called the Euky Bear. Age reporter Henrietta Cook was burnt using the Euky Bear. Simon Schluter An image of a blonde, sleeping baby graced the cardboard packaging, alongside the words “natural all-night relief from the symptoms of cold and flu”. I never experienced that relief. Instead, it became a powerful case study that illustrated failures with the sales and oversight of medical devices – the very issues currently being exposed by this masthead’s Medical Malfunctions investigation. The $58 device didn’t have an off button, and I worried it would overheat and start a fire once all the water had evaporated. I was also left with a large, painful burn on my chest after my skin brushed the humidifier’s hot plastic while I was trying to turn it off one night. It took months to fade. I was one of the lucky ones.

Advertisement

Data scraped off the Therapeutic Goods Administration’s website as part of this masthead’s investigation into the toll of medical devices shows that Australians have lodged 55 adverse reports about the Euky Bear since 2021. At least two babies required skin grafts to treat third-degree burns after touching the device. “Infant suffered a full thickness burn to two fingers requiring a skin graft,” one 2022 report from a consumer read. In another incident, a parent reported that their Euky Bear had overheated and smoked out their baby’s bedroom with a “dangerous, plastic smell”. That baby was rushed to hospital after inhaling the smoke. For years, the company received a steady stream of complaints about its device. Dozens of disgruntled customers took to the Euky Bear Facebook page to report their terrifying experiences. One mother wrote that her vaporiser had started to smoke and almost caused a fire. Many raised concerns about the residue in the base of their vaporiser after use, and believed this was linked to the heating element disintegrating.

Advertisement

In September 2023, about 10 months after my dangerous encounter with the vaporiser, the company issued a partial recall of four batches of the Euky Bear. It said this was due to a “quality issue” that could lead to the heating element overheating. My device was not among the four batches of the EBSV2013 model of the vaporiser that had been recalled. I’m not someone who puts up with shoddy products, so I fired off an email to the manufacturer, Felton Grimwade & Bosisto’s Pty Ltd. In the October 6, 2023 email, I asked for a refund and explained that the device had burnt me. If it had burnt me, what sort of damage might it inflict on a young child? I also sounded the alarm about black sediment which accumulated at the bottom of the device after each use. “It is coming from the heating module which appears to be disintegrating,” I wrote. The pharmacy where I purchased the device arranged for it to be sent to the manufacturer for safety testing.

Advertisement

A month later, the manufacturer emailed me to say that the device was “operating per normal”. “We are confident that the vaporiser does not pose a risk, if used in accordance with the operating manual,” the customer services team wrote. They informed me that it would report my concerns to the Therapeutic Goods Administration. Loading I sent a quick response: “Thank you for running these tests on the machine. Unfortunately I will never feel safe using it again after my experience.” The Euky Bear remained in my garage, gathering dust, and I didn’t think of it again until an image of the device flashed up on my colleague Liam Mannix’s computer screen at work earlier this year. We were investigating medical devices, and our web scrapers were busily making sense of thousands of adverse event reports made to the TGA. This device had caught Mannix’s attention because its manufacturers had issued the partial recall of a device model, only to issue a full recall of the same model 18 months later.

Advertisement

We wondered how often that happened and if the device manufacturer was trying to minimise the damage with a partial recall before the tide of adverse reports became too much to ignore. Related Article Exclusive

Medical Malfunctions ‘Race to the bottom’: Investigation reveals toll of weak medical device laws Mannix’s sleuthing also alerted me that my Euky Bear, which had previously been declared safe by the manufacturers, had since been recalled. The company – despite having my email – never contacted me to tell me the device was now unsafe for sale. The recall notice on the TGA website, published in May 2025, spoke to many of the concerns raised by customers. The device’s heating module could disintegrate, produce smoke, and the exposed element could cause severe burns. Consumers were told to stop using the device, cut its power cord and dispose of it. While Euky Bear’s sponsor also cancelled its registration in March 2026, I was still able to purchase the device online almost two months later. On May 4, I bought another Euky Bear from Dick Smith, which was selling the device on behalf of a company called Cosmetics Squad. While this second humidifier was a newer model not caught up in the recalls, it should have never been sold because it was no longer registered as a medical device. It is a criminal offence to supply, sell or import unregistered medical devices in Australia. The offence carries financial penalties and even imprisonment.

title: Port guide: Papeete, French Polynesia
text:
Advertisement

InspirationCruises Port guide: Papeete, French Polynesia Brian Johnston July 20, 2026 — 11:39am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

This article is part of Traveller’s ultimate guide to cruise ports. See all stories .

This modest port may be drab and unexciting, but it’s the gateway to one of the world’s most stunning, must-visit cruise regions. Who goes there Papeete is visited by the larger ships of companies such as Norwegian Cruise Line, Princess Cruises and Royal Caribbean during extended South Pacific journeys. It’s also the departure and arrival port for French Polynesia cruises operated by luxury lines such as Ponant, Silversea and Windstar. Mixed freight-passenger company Aranui and Paul Gauguin (now owned by Ponant) are locally based. Sail on in Papeete – the gateway to one of the world’s most stunning, must-visit cruise regions. iStock

Advertisement

On a French Polynesia cruise, you might well arrive in the dark following a short hop from Moorea. During the sail away, however, the steep green mountains that rise behind Papeete and the lagoon that fronts it redeem the town. That said, you’ll be getting far more spectacular approaches and departures at every other island. Sign up for the Traveller Deals newsletter Get exclusive travel deals delivered straight to your inbox. Sign up now. Berth rites Te Anuanua Cruise Terminal, opened in early 2025, has brought organisation and polish to a formerly dishevelled experience. It has ample seating, luggage storage, and stalls selling handicrafts. Wander upstairs to the cultural space and exhibition room to see what’s happening. If you’re peckish, the surrounding areas have several restaurants. Before and after Travellers will likely have to overnight in Papeete before their cruise. Resort hotels sit on the edge of the lagoon beyond town. Hilton Hotel Tahiti has an agreeable lagoon-style pool and views towards Moorea and because it lies between the town and airport, is favoured by cruise companies. InterContinental Tahiti Resort & Spa is also close to the airport, but further from town. There are cheaper but uninspiring hotel options in Papeete itself.

Advertisement

Going ashore Papeete’s town centre is full of markets and shops. French Polynesia is ravishing, but its gateway capital unfortunately anything but – and hot and humid too. Only the coconut palms and bougainvillea soften its edges. Still, the waterfront is pleasant, and the town full of markets and shops. If you’re after Polynesian black pearls, this is your opportunity. Those with more time should explore beyond town. Don’t miss The Museum of Tahiti and the Islands, 15 kilometres from Papeete, is the best museum in French Polynesia and has sumptuous views across the strait towards Moorea from extensive gardens. It has exhibits on geology, flora, fauna and handicrafts, and you can learn more about customs such as tattooing and tapa-weaving. It also does a good job of explaining Polynesia’s pre-European history and the impact of colonialisation.

Advertisement

Related Article Tahiti There’s more to this paradise than just overwater bungalows Get active Tu Marama Promenade links several squares and parks along the waterfront. You’ll pass Paofai Gardens, which has outdoor exercise equipment. Otherwise, you can jet ski or sail in the harbour. Tahiti’s rugged interior is splendid, but you’ll need a full day for four-wheel-drive tours that might include rappelling down the side of waterfalls or crawling through lava tubes. Best bites Papeete residents are fond of food trucks or roulottes and, given the high cost of dining, so are visitors. You’ll find several outside the cruise terminal, and more along the waterfront. They might serve anything from coffee and firi firi (the local doughnut) to full meals. They generally offer Polynesian, French or Chinese cuisine. Two dishes to try are pua’a roti (glazed roast pork) and poisson cru (raw fish marinated in lime juice and coconut milk). Most roulottes are cash only.

Advertisement

Further afield The most popular excursion takes cruise passengers on a drive around the road that circumnavigates most of the island. You’ll stop in places such as Matavai Bay for the view, Point Venus associated with Captain James Cook, and the Water Gardens of Vaipahi. Alternatively, a four-wheel-drive excursion will take you into the lush Papenoo Valley, which has archaeological sites and waterfalls.

title: The World Cup is over. Here's where your football fix comes from next
text:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup might be over, but the beautiful game lives on, and there's plenty more football to sink your teeth into.

A cumulative 19 million Australians have tuned in to watch the World Cup over the course of the tournament, among them new fans who might be searching for a new team or a new hero player to follow.

Australia's top-flight A League men's competition will resume on 16 October.

Here's what's coming up before then.

EU and US comps underway and to come

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

As the northern hemisphere's summer draws to an end, players return to the fields and punters fill the stands in national competitions.

In Europe, Norway's Eliteserien is already in its sixth round and will run until December, with players such as Patrick Berg having almost no time to recover after making the World Cup quarter-finals in a historic run.

Fans hoping to get more of the charismatic Erling Haaland will have to wait until the English Premier League (EPL) kicks off on 21 August.

Haaland plays for Manchester City, along with the World Cup-winning Spanish captain Rodri, who was also awarded the tournament's best player.

Spain's La Liga starts on 15 August.

This star-studded comp is where you can watch 19-year-old Lamine Yamal, crucial goalscorer Ferran Torres, and Pedri take the field for Barcelona off the back of their iconic win against Argentina.

The Socceroos' Mat Ryan is expected to take the pitch with Levante while France's two-time World Cup Golden Boot winner, Kylian Mbappé, plays for Real Madrid.

The Italian Serie A and the French Ligue 1 will both start on 23 August.

In Italy we can expect to see Socceroos Alessandro Circati and Christian Volpato play for Parma and Sassuolo respectively.

The United States' Major League Soccer tournament has already started — tune in there to watch living legend Lionel Messi and fellow Argentine Rodrigo De Paul play for Inter Miami and Australia's Lucas Herrington make an impact with the Colorado Rapids.

Champions League

The European UEFA Champions League is considered the most prestigious annual tournament in international football.

The 36-team league is made up of the best clubs on the continent, and it's one way you can watch all of your World Cup favourites battle it out, even when they play in different national club competitions.

READ MORE All the records broken during the 2026 FIFA World Cup

Qualifying rounds are already underway with the league phase starting 8 September.

Women's World Cup countdown begins

The 2026 FIFA World Cup is over, but the build-up to the 2027 Women's World Cup is underway.

Hosted by Brazil, the Matildas have already qualified for a spot in the July 2027 tournament while other qualifying matches are continuing this year.

Women's club competitions start in the US and Europe shortly after the local men's tournaments.

Many of the Matildas including Steph Catley, Kyra Cooney-Cross and Mary Fowler play in the English Women's Super League, which has its first match on 4 September.

title: WATCH: Super-sub Ferran Torres' goal wins Spain second World Cup
text:
In Brief Spain are the World Cup champions for the second time in the team's history.

Argentina have finished second after they were unable to put a score on the ledger.

Spain have seized the World Cup from the hands of defending champions Argentina after a late strike from Ferran Torres to seal a 1-0 victory in the New Jersey final.

Riding the momentum of their record-breaking fourth 2024 European Championship victory, Spain have etched their names into football history with a legacy win — taking home the trophy of all trophies for the second time, after their iconic 2010 World Cup triumph and making them the first nation in football history to hold the men's and women's World Cup titles at the same time.

The team remains the only nation ever to win the European Championship and World Cup in successive tournaments, having now done so a second time after their Euro 2024 title. They are also the first World Cup champion ever to concede only once across an entire tournament, and they are the first winner of the UEFA Nations League to capture a World title, having captured the eight-year-old competition in 2022-2023.

"We've won everything, everything," said Spain manager Luis de la Fuente through an interpreter. "And that's wonderful, with a generation of footballers, I'll say it again and again, who are an example for Spanish sport, for Spanish youth, for Spain. Together we are stronger, let there be no doubt about that."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

For Argentina and skipper Lionel Messi — despite showing pluck and courage until the whistle — it was a somewhat disappointing end to a World Cup campaign that until this match showed they were full of surprises.

The Argentines had been dreaming of a fourth World Cup and a back-to-back trophy after winning against France at Qatar in 2022— but it was not to be.

Spain appeared to dominate for much of the game with Argentina barely getting a look in, but it was only in extra time that one of Spain's dozens of shots broke through.

In the 106th minute, well into extra time, Ferran Torres managed to land a strike into the roof of the net, just as a penalty shootout felt all but assured.

Torres was a late-game sub, taking the pitch in the 62nd minute.

"It was a goal scored by 47 million people, it wasn’t even mine or from the 26 players. It was destined to happen; it was ‌meant to be a winner," Torres said, speaking after the win.

It was 27C and sunny for the 80,000-strong crowd at New York New Jersey Stadium but it took close to full time for gameplay to heat up on the pitch.

After 49 minutes of bitsy, defensive play, the two sides were locked on 0-0 when they cleared the pitch for the half-time show. The score would not budge until well after the 90-minute mark.

As the clock wound down, chaos broke out around Spain's net — Messi taking back-to-back corners, Argentina making breathless attempt after attempt that they were unable to manoeuvre past Spain's formidable defensive line.

Spain dominated early, using their 67 per cent possession to show off their skills in the midfield and taunt Argentina, but the defending European champions only managed to convert three of those plays into attempts on goal going into the entertainment.

Around the five-minute mark, Lamine Yamal played a one-two in the box, collecting at the near post and getting a shot off that was saved by Emiliano Martinez.

Despite the early excitement, the first half wore on with few significant breaks. While Spain looked in control, neither side looked concerned.

After the first hydration break, Messi began dropping deeper and deeper into the midfield to pick up the play, only for Spain's defence to sweep him up every time.

The 39-year-old, eight-time Ballon d'Or winner Messi would have hoped for this final game to help him to at least nudge back into equal position with France's Kylian Mbappé as a top World Cup goal scorer.

Going into the match, the bowed-out Mbappé had 22 World Cup goals under his belt, compared to Messi's 21. But another wasn't to be, and with this largely rumoured to be his last time at the tournament, that is probably where his still mighty legacy will remain.

An apparent injury saw off Argentine centre-back Lisandro Martínez in the 43rd minute, to be replaced by Nicolás Otamendi.

The second half immediately marked a shift in play for both sides. When the teams returned from the not-quite-regulation 25-minute half-time break, they were both much livelier, but it wasn't enough to shift the game's play.

Some 43 seconds in, Spain's Alejandro Baena took an early chance on target — that was comfortably saved — before Argentina decided they had had enough of that and started taking more possession.

Despite more zest, the start-stop play that dominated the first half returned and Argentina remained defensive— the attacking side that came out of the gates swinging in the second half against England in the semi-final nowhere to be seen.

Spain continued to notch up attempts on goal, getting increasingly dangerous — but not dangerous enough — by attempt eight: A cross from Yamal offered Torres the chance to fling a header at speed into Martinez's waiting hands.

It offered a rare glimmer of excitement but it was not enough to change the pace as both teams headed into the tournament's last hydration break, increasingly tense and locked at nil-nil.

Off the back of the hydration break a frenzied feeling settled into play but not enough to change the score. Spain continued to make attempts on goal, but each one on target is saved without hesitation or fumble by Martínez.

Messi’s influence was almost completely negated time and time again by the well-organised and controlling Spanish midfield. Spain's midfield acted like a curtain until, in the 92nd minute — halfway through stoppage time— when Argentina made its first serious attack, only one of two it would make across the entire match.

As the game moved into extra time Argentina's side looked threadbare — down to just 10 men after Enzo Fernandez was sent off on a second yellow card; Messi the only attacker after Fernandez was sent off for an out-of-control tackle and Julian Alvarez came off in extra time.

The first goal of the game came well past the 90-minute mark, when Williams punted a defended ball into the net, only for it to be ruled out off the back of a penalty Mikel Merino incurred by standing on Nicolás Otamendi’s foot.

Nine minutes later, they nearly doubled their lead after Torres got a one-on-one with Martinez, but the offside flag was up, and it was thrown out.

Spain finished with 20 attempts, 12 on target, compared to Argentina's two off-target attempts.

Speaking after the game, Torres and his manager were candid about the game's slow evolution.

"Well, in the end, all finals are difficult, and when you have Messi on the opposing team, you always get nervous," Torres said.

"But ultimately, we've always depended on ourselves, on playing our style of football, and I think we demonstrated that once again today."

"Yes, I think the game should have been decided much earlier," de la Fuente added.

"Dibu's great saves prevented us from winning the game sooner. We could have taken a more comfortable lead. But this is a world championship final, and even with 10 men, you have to suffer at the end. But since we like to suffer, we've shown once again that we're ready for anything."

Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain were the better side but said he was proud of his side.

READ MORE All the records broken during the 2026 FIFA World Cup

"They were better, that’s the truth. But I’ll hold on to an enormous memory of what they’ve done, of what it’s worth to get this far," he said.

"We're great in victory, and we have to be great in defeat.

"Today we're showing that we know how to lose. We lost the match, and we're owning it.

"But that doesn’t mean we’ll stop remembering everything we did to get here.

Scaloni said he hadn't spoken with Messi about his future, but his tournament had been "unbelievable".

"He's 39 years old now," Scaloni said.

"I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he's achieved, because he's the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. I have no doubt about this, and what he did during this World Cup was incredible. But also what he did before this."

— With additional reporting by Reuters news agency.

title: 'Impossible': Nauru community detainees say Australian funds not enough to buy food with
text:
IN BRIEF Community detainees in Nauru say they are not being given enough money by the Australian government to live on.

99 per cent of people interviewed said they don't have enough money for food.

Asylum seekers being detained within the community in Nauru are struggling to buy enough food and are being forced to choose between eating and paying for other necessities.

The difficulties are being driven by a mix of high living costs and low income support, according to a new report by the Asylum Seeker Resource Centre.

Of 78 people interviewed for the report, 99 per cent said they couldn't afford to buy enough food with their current allowance.

On top of that, 68 per cent said they rarely or never had access to fresh fruit, vegetables and meat, and 82 per cent said they didn't have easy access to free, clean drinking water.

Community detainees live on $260 a fortnight to cover all of their basic needs outside accommodation.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

The group have been sent to the tiny island nation under Australia's long-standing policy of offshore processing for asylum-seekers who arrive by boat.

Speaking anonymously, detainees consistently told the centre the allowance wasn't enough to sustain them, forcing them to decide between buying food and other necessities like clothing and internet access.

READ MORE Hundreds of former detainees to be deported to Nauru set to live among community

Many were running out of money before their next payment and were forced to reduce meals or go without food altogether.

"With food we have to compromise, if you buy vegetables you can't afford rice. If you buy meat, you can't afford fruit," one said.

"The money is not enough because everything is expensive here — rice, fish, we cannot buy any fruit here, internet for $100 per month, how is it possible to survive?" said another.

Nauru struggles with food insecurity due to its remote location and limited local agriculture, with perishable food flown in weekly and often selling out quickly or being prohibitively expensive.

With no rivers or freshwater lakes, the island also relies primarily on rainwater harvesting and desalination, meaning water shortages are common during times of low rainfall, forcing those who live there to turn to bottled water.

Asylum seekers in the community often struggle due to their lack of orientation or local knowledge as to how food systems work, as well as infrequent transportation to and from markets, the resource centre said.

As of 31 May, there were 101 people seeking asylum in Nauru, 91 of whom were in community accommodation.

The majority of those in detention in Nauru were young to middle-aged men from south and east Asia.

The group of asylum seekers are separate from a group of hundreds of former immigration detainees, known as the NZYQ cohort, sent to Nauru from Australia after the High Court in 2024 ruled indefinite detention to be unlawful.

title: Vincent has never left the US. Three hours in Australia are about to change that
text:
Vincent Simmons has never left the United States before. But in a few days, he'll board a 15-hour flight to Melbourne. Not for the beaches, the coffee or the art — but for a cinema.

Splitting the cost across a few credit cards, the 35-year-old Los Angeles projectionist has put close to $4,000 into flights and accommodation for the three-week trip, all to watch The Odyssey — the latest film from Oscar-winning director Christopher Nolan, released in Australia on 16 July — at Melbourne's IMAX.

He'll see it there twice — on top of a screening back home in LA just before he flies out.

"I've heard about the IMAX in Melbourne for years — I've probably watched every YouTube video that shows even a sliver of it," Simmons tells SBS News. "I told myself I'd go one day."

"70mm IMAX offers hands down the greatest sensory engrossment you could possibly have when watching a film," he explains.

Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"It's the closest sense of lifelike fidelity — a transcendent film format that everyone should experience at least once in their life. But to be fair, if they experience it once, they will want to experience it again."

Melbourne's IMAX theatre is the only one in the Southern Hemisphere capable of showing this film as the director intended — and fans are flying from around the world to see it. Source: Supplied / Eugene Hyland

He calls the trip his own odyssey.

"I was brave enough to undertake my own odyssey in travelling to Melbourne," he says.

"Hopefully it won't be as burdensome as Odysseus' journey!"

Not any old cinema

Melbourne's IMAX isn't just any old cinema.

It's one of only 41 in the world equipped to project true IMAX 70mm film, the largest film stock in existence — so large that the camera has to be reloaded every three minutes during a shoot.

At 32 metres wide and 23 metres tall, and with an aspect ratio of 1.43:1, the Melbourne IMAX's screen is also the biggest of its kind anywhere.

Jeremy Fee says the Melbourne IMAX is the only place in the Southern Hemisphere film-lovers can see The Odyssey as Christopher Nolan intended. Source: Supplied / Eugene Hyland

"Because we're only one of 41 cinemas in the entire world that can screen this film on IMAX 70mm on our large 1.43:1 aspect ratio screen, it's a place that customers seek out," Jeremy Fee, general manager at IMAX Melbourne, tells SBS News.

"This is the only place in Australia, in the southern hemisphere, that you can see it in the way that Christopher Nolan intended."

Just seven of those theatres are outside of North America.

The film print itself is a "massive beast", Fee says.

Laid out end to end, a single print of The Odyssey stretches more than 17km.

Only five people in Australia are qualified to handle the 70mm film reels that Odysseus will be projected from at IMAX Melbourne. Source: Supplied / Eugene Hyland

Its reels run on platters so large they can hold three hours of runtime — just eight minutes more than The Odyssey's own two hours and 52 minutes.

Each session requires constant quality assurance checks to keep the film running true — and just five people in Australia are currently qualified to do it.

"It's a perishable skill," he says. "It was [a dying craft] … I think it's a growing art form now."

While a renewed appreciation for the craft may have averted — or at least delayed — its demise, the current scarcity of those accredited to carry it out is exactly what's pulling people across oceans to watch a movie in Melbourne.

An odyssey in itself

At Melbourne's IMAX opening weekend for The Odyssey, it's a similar story up and down the queue: people have travelled a long way to be here.

Kaivalya, a 28-year-old PhD student and Nolan-fanatic, is in the middle of relocating to Melbourne from Maharashtra, India — but pushed his flights forward by a month so he could catch The Odyssey early.

It's not the first time cinema has dictated his travel plans; in 2014, he travelled 400km from his hometown in India to see Nolan's Interstellar on the nearest IMAX screen he could find.

Now, Melbourne's IMAX is about to become his local.

"I intend to watch many movies in this theatre in the coming months," he says. "It will be my go-to destination for movies for the next year."

Kaivalya moved to Australia an entire month earlier than originally planned so he could see The Odyssey during its opening weekend. Source: SBS News

His eyes light up when he talks about why film wins out over digital.

"This is a much more immersive experience because the resolution is much higher," he says. "The colours feel warmer. I love watching the grain in the movie — it gives it life."

His friends back home are also chasing a similar experience.

"My friends in India are travelling internationally — some to China, some to the US. The nearest IMAX 70mm theatre to India is in Thailand, so many people are travelling there and applying for visas," he says.

"It's like a concert."

Mitchell and Dimeth, both 22, didn't travel as far — a weekend trip down from Brisbane — but they've built an entire ritual around seeing Nolan's films at the Melbourne cinema.

"We came last time to see Oppenheimer," Mitchell tells SBS News. "It's kind of our thing — for Nolan, we'll come and watch it here."

He was online the moment general tickets dropped last month: "I waited an hour to make sure we got good tickets," he says.

Their closest IMAX, on the Gold Coast, is digital and smaller.

Friends and hardcore Nolan fans Mitchell and Dimeth have travelled from Brisbane to Melbourne multiple times to watch the renowned director's films. Source: SBS News

Dimeth says their closest IMAX on the Gold Coast, which is smaller and runs digitally, doesn't compare to the scale and theatre of Melbourne.

"This level of immersion isn't something you get in many other cinemas," he says.

Mitchell reckons the hype has only made the tickets more valuable.

"I think people are jealous that we get to see it," he says. "Someone I work with was trying to buy a ticket off me."

A tourist attraction

Just seven weeks of sessions have been released so far, with months of screenings still to come. But records are expected to be broken.

"We're up to 52,500 people who have bought tickets to The Odyssey so far in this cinema," Fee says, adding that each session can only seat 450 people.

Box office for Odyssey at the Melbourne IMAX is already well past $2 million, rapidly closing in on the cinema's all-time record of $2.78 million set by another Nolan film, Oppenheimer. It's currently the 7th highest-grossing film of all time in the theatre — with no signs of slowing down.

"We're so close to already knocking that one over," Fee says.

"We've got people coming from Germany, Turkey, New Zealand, India, Sri Lanka … I've never seen the amount of hype for a release as I have for this one."

READ MORE This film starring Rose Byrne airs the unspeakable parts of motherhood

But there are still challenges — and cinemas like Melbourne's can't simply be replicated elsewhere.

The film projection system was pulled from the cinema in 2015 and sat dormant for two years before being reinstalled in 2017 at Nolan's request for his film Dunkirk.

"You have to invest a lot of money," Fee says.

"For Oppenheimer, there were 30 film projector sites for IMAX. They managed to find another 11 projectors for The Odyssey to get it up to 41. I don't know if there's any more out there, and they're not building any more."

"So it's a really lucky and fortuitous thing that we still have our film projection system — and that those 40 other theatres across the globe have held onto theirs as well."

Fee expects the same scenes to play out again in December, when Denis Villeneuve's Dune: Part Three — also shot substantially on IMAX 70mm — opens on 17 December.

For now, though, Melbourne has an unlikely tourist attraction on its hands.

"It's a tourism boom for Melbourne, for us to be able to have this here," he says. "The fact that people are building their holidays and their trips around coming to Melbourne is super exciting."

"It's changed cinema into this event."

title: Spain beat Argentina 1-0 to win World Cup final — as it happened
text:
Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain were the better side but said he was proud of his side.

Argentine coach Lionel Scaloni said Spain were the better side but he was proud of his men. Source: Getty / Alex Livesey

"They were better, that’s the truth. But I’ll hold on to an enormous memory of what they’ve done, of what it’s worth to get this far," he said.

"We're great in victory, and we have to be great in defeat.

"Today we're showing that we know how to lose. We lost the match, and we're owning it.

"But that doesn’t mean we’ll stop remembering everything we did to get here.

Scaloni said he hadn't spoken with Messi about his future, but his tournament had been "unbelievable".

"He's 39 years old now," Scaloni said.

"It was crystal clear for me that he was going to play up until he decided not to.

"I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he's achieved, because he's the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. I have no doubt about this, and what he did during this World Cup was incredible. But also what he did before this."

— Miles Proust

title: Hanson tells UK conservative conference audience to 'not apologise for being white'
text:
In brief Hanson told a UK crowd that Australia shouldn't introduce cultures "incompatible" with the local way of life.

Australian politicians have criticised Hanson's recent comments as un-Australian and unfair.

Don't apologise for being white, Pauline Hanson has said at a conservative conference in London amid a backdrop of growing backlash over her comments on migration and transgender rights.

Speaking at the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) on Saturday morning UK time, the One Nation leader told a sparse crowd that Australia had been "flooded" with migrants and gone "completely woke" over transgender rights.

Her speech capped a high-profile European trip that included a podcast interview with English anti-immigration activist and convicted criminal Tommy Robinson and an appearance at the Dolce & Gabbana fashion show in Italy with billionaire One Nation donor Gina Rinehart.

"I'm getting feedback from Australia, from the media, saying, 'How dare I associate with Tommy Robinson, how dare I speak to him,'" Hanson told a conference room that, judging by footage posted online, looked less than one-third full.

"Do not apologise for being white. I'm sick of hearing about white privilege."

Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Hanson also said Australia was "too stupid" to get its laws around discrimination against transgender people right and praised a UK court decision that ruled the legal definition of a woman depended on their biological sex.

"There's going to be so much psychological damage that's going to be done to our future generations," she said.

Hanson had already come under fire in Australia earlier in the week for her interview with Robinson, in which she pointed to the scrapping of the White Australia policy as the root cause of migration issues in the country.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese addressed those comments on Saturday, saying it was important to remember that the racially discriminatory immigration policy was dismantled by both sides of politics and challenged the Opposition leader to rule out any future political deals with One Nation.

Hanson later released a video on social media saying allegations that she wanted to reintroduce the White Australia policy were false. "Never, ever have I advocated that, and I don't believe in that," she said.

"Yes, I've been strong on immigration. Bringing the right people into the country in a measured way and people who want to assimilate and be Australians."

'It's farcical'

Other speakers at the London CPAC event included Nationals senator Bridget McKenzie, Reform UK leader Nigel Farage and short-lived former Tory prime minister Liz Truss.

After an earlier meeting with Farage fell over due to his resignation from the UK parliament to re-contest his seat, Hanson and the Reform leader met briefly on the sidelines of the conference.

Hanson also told the conference that Australia had brought in too many immigrants, and this was putting too much pressure on the housing system.

She also said migrants should want to be Australian and not introduce cultures that were "incompatible" with the Australian way of life.

"Do I want Sharia Law? No way in the whole wide world," she said.

"Do I want child marriages? No."

"Do I want the burqa seen on our streets. No."

Gamel Kheir from the Lebanese Muslim Association — who spoke to SBS News on Saturday about similar comments Hanson made during her Tommy Robinson interview — challenged the One Nation leader to provide evidence of such claims.

"If she's going to throw that sort of slur that we somehow practice Sharia law, where is the evidence for that?" he said.

"I have yet to meet anyone Muslim or non-Muslim who advocates for their own law in contrast and in competition with the Australian law. It's farcical."

Backlash grows

Asked about Hanson's remarks, Liberal moderate and frontbencher Andrew Bragg said Hanson was going out of her way to make life harder for minorities.

"The idea that there's a spectre of transgender people taking over Australia, I just think is insane," he told ABC's Insiders program on Sunday.

However, he refused to be drawn on whether the Coalition should rule out a potential preference deal with One Nation at the next federal election.

Instead, he extended his criticism to the government, insisting that picking between Labor and One Nation would be difficult as both were "terrible".

In early June, polling emerged that suggested One Nation was Australia's most popular political party. While more recent surveys have indicated a drop in primary support for One Nation, the party remains ahead of the Coalition.

Deputy Liberal leader Jane Hume told Sky News on Sunday that Hanson's comments about the White Australia policy were "unnecessary", "divisive", "inflammatory" and "totally un-Australian".

She said that, in her discussions with Australians, no-one had brought up the racially discriminatory immigration scheme or the idea of banning the burqa.

NSW Premier Chris Minns added that it was "blatantly unfair" and untrue to blame non-white migrants for any problems that existed in Australia, adding that any attempt to return to past discriminatory policies would undermine the nation's standing.

"That would make Australia a pariah nation, probably one of the only in the world that had that kind of race-based immigration policies," he said.

title: Sydney family embrace divided loyalties ahead of Argentina-Spain World Cup showdown
text:
IN BRIEF The World Cup final between Spain and Argentina kicks off at 5am Monday morning (AEST).

While Spain has only won the cup once before, Argentina is vying for its fourth victory.

Whatever the result in the FIFA World Cup final, it will be a win-win for Sydney couple Sandra Hurtado and Fernando Travasso.

On Monday morning (AEST), Spaniard Hurtado will be wearing a La Roja jersey while her partner, Travasso, will be backing his native Argentina as the two sides meet at New York New Jersey Stadium.

Their three children — twins Sofia and Carla, 10, and Marco, 11 — have grown up with both cultures. While that makes for some divided loyalties in the household, their parents stress that there's no bad blood.

"For me, especially, it feels like the cup is already home because whoever wins doesn't matter for us," Hurtado told SBS News.

"Whoever wins is a win for us ... I'm gonna celebrate anyway."

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

Her daughter Sofia also doesn't mind which way the results go.

"I just want both to win, and even if one of them loses, I think they can improve next time and get better," she said.

Despite the Argentine jerseys, siblings Carla, Sofia and Marco (left to right) say they don't mind which team wins. Source: SBS News

"As both of my siblings said, it's very hard to choose."

A 'great excuse to be even more connected'

Hurtado and Travasso arrived in Australia in 2008 and have spent almost two decades building a life together while keeping their Spanish and Argentine traditions alive.

Travasso said the World Cup had become another way to stay connected to family on the other side of the world.

"I think it's a great excuse to be even more connected," he said.

"We've been heavily involved in the community [and] integrated into Australian society, but we still manage to keep our identity and [pass on] the values and history of each of our cultures.

"Every day we have conversations with our relatives. When the World Cup comes ... and we have both countries together ... we're going to be using WhatsApp ... at the same time."

The final also promises some friendly rivalry at home.

"Once we sit down there with our different T-shirts and all the things, I still think that would be a nice competition, it would be a little bit spicy, and we are going to have good fun," Travasso said.

Growing up in Spain, Hurtado said football was deeply embedded in the culture.

"In Spain, for us, football is the same as AFL [is for Australia]," she said.

READ MORE Argentina are one win from glory — and fans are turning to lucky rituals

"Spain has amazing football players, and we have amazing teams that are very good [in the European tournaments]."

She said that, before winning the World Cup in 2010, Spain had "never made it too far".

"Hopefully now, we're gonna win."

'It's part of the identity'

While Spain is chasing its second title, Argentina has a chance for its fourth.

It would also be a second consecutive championship for Messi's side after their 2022 win — a feat that would make it the first nation to go back-to-back since Brazil in 1962.

Like Spain, football is more than just a sport in Argentina.

"It's part of the identity," Travasso said.

"Through different cities in Argentina, you can find big murals or big paintings on the wall with [Lionel] Messi.

"A few memories that I have when I was little ... were a TV, white and black, with [Diego] Maradona playing."

Hurtado said she hopes her children take away something more important than the result.

"I think Argentinians are the same [as Spanish]," she said.

"In life it's not about one person, it's about a team. When someone wins, it's not just the person who scores the goal; it's everybody working together towards the same goal.

"That's what we're trying to [teach our children]."

title: Could Alzheimer’s be predicted years before symptoms? A test offers new clues
text:
IN BRIEF The so-called p-tau217 test had previously been used only on those with symptoms.

Some experts are already calling it a "game changer".

What if a simple test could reveal your risk of Alzheimer's years before memory problems start?

A new study on a specific blood test shows this might be possible.

The test looks for what doctors call 'p-tau217' and can alert them to the disease five to 10 years before a healthy person develops symptoms.

That information could be reassuring or terrifying, but for now, it's a potential tool to speed drug development by helping to identify and enrol high-risk people into studies of possible Alzheimer's treatments or preventive strategies.

The scientists behind the study, Reisa Sperling from the Mass General Brigham Neuroscience Institute in the US, said the so-called p-tau217 test had previously been used only on those with symptoms.

News that makes sense Your trusted source for staying up-to-date with the world around you. Get free daily news updates and analysis, straight to your inbox. Enter email address Subscribe Morning (Mon–Fri) Afternoon (Mon–Fri) Weekend Insight By subscribing, you agree to SBS’s terms of service and privacy policy including receiving email updates from SBS.

"Right now it's really being utilised in people who already have symptoms of Alzheimer's disease and are thought to have cognitive impairment due to Alzheimer's disease," she said.

"In this study, we are looking at people who do not yet have symptoms of Alzheimer's disease."

How does the Alzheimer's test work?

The results, presented at the Alzheimer's Association International Conference in London, showed similar patterns in those at risk of developing Alzheimer’s.

It found that symptom-free older adults who harboured very high levels of p-tau217 in the blood test had a 38 per cent risk of developing cognitive impairment over five years. That risk grew to 78 per cent by 10 years.

"We also found that people who have a very low level of this blood test, p-tau217, were very unlikely to progress over time and were about the same level as people in general as they age over a 10-year period might be at risk for developing impairment," Sperling said

READ MORE My mum is living her worst fear; I just found out I have the same gene

She said the blood test data offers some new clues. While different intermediate levels of p-tau217 signalled progressive risk, only the very highest level seemed to correlate with other evidence about that tipping point.

Researchers analysed data from 2,684 healthy older adults enrolled in long-running Alzheimer's studies since 2004, all of whom took the test when they joined. By last year, 478 of them had developed cognitive impairment.

A 'game changer'

The test can reveal a person's risk years in advance, information that may be reassuring for some and distressing for others, but it does not offer a cure.

"At this point, it wouldn't change what I would tell someone to do. I'd still tell them to eat well, sleep well, exercise a lot and stay engaged. But that's going to change dramatically if the current prevention trials show clinical benefit," Sperling said.

Already, large clinical trials are testing whether certain drugs could prevent or at least delay the disease.

If any of those works, doctors should have an easy way to tell who should try them — and the blood test can be exactly what they need.

That is why Jessica Langbaum from the Banner Alzheimer’s Institute based in the US, explained the development as a "game changer" for neary 10 people who are diagnosed with Alzheimer's every year.

"This is really important for people, particularly if we get to a time when there's one of the treatments that are being tested for Alzheimer's prevention therapies," she said.

"If one of those treatments read out having a blood test that can accurately determine who to test and say, yes, this person might be eligible for treatment, I think that's a real game changer."

— With additional reporting by the Associated Press
