title: Moon landing: Why this Chinese AI model rattled global sharemarkets
text:
Advertisement

TechnologyAI Moon landing: Why this Chinese AI model rattled global sharemarkets Tim Biggs July 20, 2026 — 11:50am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

China’s Moonshot AI, co-founded by former Google and Meta researcher Yang Zhilin, rattled western markets last week with the release of an impressive new model called Kimi K3. Now, the company reportedly plans to list publicly with a valuation of more than $43 billion. The company says its open-weight AI model outperforms all rivals with the exception of the most powerful; Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI’s GPT-5.6. Demand for the massive 2.8 trillion parameter model was so huge over the weekend that Moonshot had to temporarily pause sign-ups. Users have enthused about its coding and natural language capabilities, and compared its long context and efficiency favourable to the most popular US-developed models. Moonshot co-founder Yang Zhilin has a PhD and previously worked at Google, Meta and Huawei. Getty On release last week, a global sell-off affecting chip-makers and big tech wiped out trillions in market capitalisation. Bloomberg and others are now reporting that Moonshot has advised investors it hit annual recurring revenue of $US300 million ($430 million) in June, and is wrapping up a funding round that may value it at more than $US30 billion ($43 billion), ahead of a listing in as early as six months. Though still in his early thirties, Zhilin has a PhD in computer science from Carnegie Mellon University, is a serial startup founder and has worked at Google, Meta and Huawei. He co-founded Moonshot in 2023. It has largely lived in the shadow of other Chinese AI startups DeepSeek and Z.AI before now, though its long-memory chatbot Kimi began attracting hype earlier this year. With K3, the company has burst into mainstream relevance.

Advertisement

Related Article China relations Not a solo performance, but a symphony: What China’s leader thinks about AI The model is the best-performing open-weight technology yet to emerge from China. Open-weight refers to an AI model where the trained files are available for anyone to download. It’s not a fully open source, in the sense that users don’t necessarily have insight into how the model was trained. But open weight models (like Moonshot’s K3, and Meta’s Llama) differ significantly from the closed offerings of the big tech companies because anyone can run, host or customise them. OpenAI, Anthropic and Google only offer access to their models through paid APIs. The market reaction to Moonshot’s news mirrors the shockwaves we’ve seen previously whenever a Chinese lab makes a major AI breakthrough, with temporary sell-offs in Western semiconductor and AI tech stocks, as investors reconsider assumptions about how wide the US lead in AI technology actually is. However, Moonshot’s move is quite unlike the early DeepSeek releases that previously prompted a knee-jerk panic. While DeepSeek’s focus on ultra-low prices stoked fears of a democratisation and rapid commoditisation, Moonshot shows that the open-source gap has shrunk faster than expected. Developers now have access to frontier-class reasoning and coding capabilities, without needing to lock themselves into closed APIs. The company’s reported revenue shows that businesses are willing to pay to build on it.

Advertisement

Editor's pick Analysis

AI AI-powered Siri is on the way – but is it worth using? But that’s not to say Moonshot is cheaper or easier to run than any other mid-tier enterprise AI, including the big western ones. K3 is a massive model that requires serious hardware to host; you can’t simply download and run it on a regular PC. Consumer users will go through Moonshot’s Kimi chatbot to access the model, and that comes with a tiered subscription. Enterprises and developers will rent access to servers or custom installations, and the Chinese company’s API pricing is in line with competitors. Of course, licensing agreements stop another company installing K3 and on-selling access without giving Moonshot a cut. History suggests the market will settle as investors realise that a rising tide lifts all boats, and that increased competition and a multi-polar market for AI may ultimately be good for the likes of Nvidia, TSMC and even rival AI firms. But K3 has proven that open models can rival closed ones when used for high-value tasks, like coding and agents. title: First home buyer nabs Croydon house sold by investor for $1.83m
text:
Advertisement

PropertyNewsAuctions First home buyer nabs Croydon house sold by investor for $1.83m Carmen Forward July 20, 2026 — 11:50am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A charming two-bedroom home in Croydon was offloaded by an investor and snapped up by a first home buyer for $1,832,000 at auction on Saturday. The full brick single level home at 49 Wetherill Street came with dramatic fireplaces, an updated bathroom and a spacious backyard with covered outdoor area. The property was one of 564 scheduled auctions in Sydney last week. By Saturday evening, Domain Group recorded a preliminary auction clearance rate of 49 per cent from 325 reported results, while 114 auctions were withdrawn. Withdrawn auctions are counted as unsold properties when calculating the clearance rate. Bidding for the Croydon home opened at its $1.6 million guide, and went up in a flurry of varying bids between three buyers.

Advertisement

Bids climbed in mostly $10,000 and $20,000 increments before slowing to smaller rises of $1000 and $2000. Related Article Auctions Edwardian-era harbourside apartment passes in at $2.2 million At $1,831,000 the property was called three times before an additional $500 each was accepted from the final two bidders. It sold under the hammer for $1,832,000. Auctioneer Tom Panos said, “most of the properties I had today had no one registered or one person registered … But for some strange reason, this property ended up getting 10 people registered on it.” Panos said the reserve was around $1.75 million.

Advertisement

“It was a good result, and it did have 10 people. But … it probably would have got that price, or even a little bit more a few months ago,” he said. “There’s a lot of vendors that are feeling distraught because they’re having to sell their properties 10 to 15 per cent lower than what they would have maybe six months, a year ago. “But I keep reminding real estate agents and their vendors: let’s not forget, most of these houses have had a 60 per cent uplift since COVID, and that is probably one of the other reasons why we’re getting this adjustment. It was an unsustainable growth.” It was sold by Lambros Markessinis from Ray White Burwood.

Advertisement

The house last traded for $185,000, in 1993, and was rented for $670 per week, records show. A five-bedroom manor in Hornsby drew 11 registrations at auction. The grand residence at 34-36 Manor Road was guided at $2.25 million. Bidding opened at $2 million and went up sporadically in a mix of $25,000, $50,000 and $10,000 bids. Eleven parties registered and six were active before a crowd.

Advertisement

When bidding reached its $2.3 million reserve the charming home was announced as on the market. It sold for $2.69 million to a builder from the area. Related Article Auctions Young family gets a $50,000 discount on stylish $2.9m Greenwich house There is no legal requirement for a vendor’s reserve to be in line with their property’s price guide. Selling agent James Campbell from Belle Property Upper North Shore said, “It’s a unique home, and because it’s on such a big block of land … it was a rare finding. “So it just brought out lots of buyers … you would have never known the market’s down.”

Advertisement

The vendors are downsizing closer to the city. In Ashfield, a six-bedroom house in need of work at 95 Holden Street, passed in at auction. The “blank canvas” guided at $2.2 million had an opening offer of $1.9 million and a further increase to $1.95 million, however the vendor did not want to sell at that price. Selling agent Michael Simpson from Ray White Ashfield and Summer Hill said two parties registered, however one did not show.

Advertisement

The property last traded for $235,000 in 1991, records show. In Blakehurst, a five-bedroom contemporary property with a swimming pool sold for $3.36 million, which was $160,000 above its $3.2 million guide and reserve. The family home at 11 Lorna Avenue had five registered buyers, all local upsizing families. Bidding opened at $3 million and four parties competed in $20,000, $10,000, $5000 and $1000 rises. Selling agent Trent Tarbey from McGrath Sans Souci said, “It was relatively timid bidding. The bidding flowed consistently … Everyone was happy to bid and get involved, but no one was stretching themselves.”

Advertisement

The vendors are upsizing to Cronulla. The home last traded for $3.09 million in 2021, records show. AMP chief economist Dr Shane Oliver said Domain’s clearance rate of 49 per cent for Sydney is “a pretty soft result”. He noted the clearance rate was “lower in the pandemic and in the 2017 to 2019 property slump.” Considering the interest rate hikes, low levels of confidence, uncertainty in the economy, and investment property tax changes, Oliver said it’s to be expected that there is a slump in the property market. “It looks to me like a normal, a cyclical downturn,” he said.

title: Joey Manu to tour PNG Chiefs set-up as latest cross-code target
text:
Advertisement

Updated SportNRLNRL 2026 Joey Manu to tour PNG Chiefs set-up as latest cross-code target Dan Walsh July 20, 2026 — 11:48am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Former Roosters star and rugby union convert Joey Manu has emerged as the PNG Chiefs’ latest marquee signing target as he tours the incoming franchise’s Port Moresby facilities. Manu was spotted at Sydney International Airport on Monday morning alongside PNG football manager Michael Chammas and head of recruitment Liam Ayoub as they travelled to the PNG capital as the club continues to build its inaugural 2028 roster. Loading Manu’s PNG sojourn is the first step in the Chiefs’ recruitment process, with the 29-year-old still signed for another year at French rugby union outfit Racing 92. Manu left rugby league and the Roosters at the height of his powers at the end of 2024 and has been regularly linked with a return to the 13-man code since.

Advertisement

He initially signed with Japanese rugby’s Toyota Verblitz before shifting to France, where he will play until June next season. PNG’s courting of Manu comes alongside continued negotiations with former NSW Origin winger and Wallabies hopeful Zac Lomax, who has been offered a multi-year deal by the Chiefs. Zac Lomax’s potential return to rugby league is gaining momentum. Getty Images Lomax is committed to seeing out the two years of his Western Force contract, having knocked back interest from Melbourne for him to return to the NRL next year, but would also come off-contract in time to feature in the Chiefs first campaign. Both he and Manu present as elite backline options for the Chiefs and offer versatility as well given Manu regularly filled in at fullback for the Roosters and New Zealand during his 181-game NRL career.

Advertisement

Joey Manu and PNG Chiefs football manager Michael Chammas at Sydney Airport on Monday. A full-time shift to the No.1 jumper was regularly discussed as a potential lure whenever Manu came off contract with the Roosters, but his ties to the Tricolours meant he never truly explored linking with an NRL rival. The Roosters were long considered Manu’s most likely destination were he to return to the NRL. However, this masthead reported in April that the club privately gave up that hope due to Manu’s earning power, with his Racing 92 salary estimated at around €1.3 million ($2 million). The Chiefs’ ability to offer tax-free wages, plus tax exemptions on any third-party agreement earnings spent in the Pacific nation, give the new franchise an ability to match overseas rugby markets that NRL rivals cannot. PNG’s pursuit of Manu and Lomax comes after their recruitment of Jarome Luai, Alex Johnston, Connor Watson and English prop Matty Lees as foundation players for 2028. The Chiefs will be able to table official offers to NRL players coming off contract from November.

Advertisement

There is no restriction on the new NRL franchise approaching potential recruits in rugby. More to come.

title: Olympic budget blowouts will be covered by private money, minister pledges
text:
Advertisement

NationalQueenslandRoad to Brisbane 2032 Olympic budget blowouts will be covered by private money, minister pledges Julius Dennis July 20, 2026 — 11:48am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Queensland’s Olympics minister says private money will pay for any potential funding blowouts for the 2032 Games, sticking to the government line no taxpayer money will be used to run the event. The pledge from Tim Mander – also the state minister of sport and racing – comes as the operational budget of the Olympic and Paralympic Games is being reviewed after organising committee boss Andrew Liveris said the $4.9 billion estimate bore “no resemblance to reality” earlier this year. The state and federal governments have committed $7.1 billion to building or upgrading 17 venues across Queensland, and the state has pencilled in a further $3.5 billion for four athletes villages. Tim Mander said delivering the first on-budget Olympics was the government’s aim. Matt Dennien Mander said the exact cost of the Games was “a little bit flexible” while the official list of sports and their venues were being nutted out, and that the governments would stick to that expenditure.

Advertisement

He said he hoped the sports were locked in by the International Olympic Committee by the end of the year. Related Article Government debt Post-COVID debt explosion a major challenge for all states, bar one “As things become more concrete, we’ll know the actual cost, and we can adjust accordingly,” he said. “Our aim is to make sure Queensland taxpayers aren’t left with the bill, and that’s one of my major objectives from an operational point of view.” When questioned on the “flexible” costs, Mander said the government would find ways to generate money.

Advertisement

“What I mean by that is it means if we’ve got to raise more money, if we’ve got to generate more ticket sales, we [will] look at other revenue-raising opportunities to meet whatever cost it might be,” he said. An artist’s impression of the proposed Brisbane Stadium, to be built at Victoria Park. The minister pointed to a Commonwealth Bank sponsorship already inked for the Olympics, which the Australian Financial Review reported to be worth $200 million, as an example of where money could come from, adding the government was on the hunt to lock in more deals. He said the government would not can regional events despite the potential savings of a more centralised event. “The state is quite electric in the regional towns about the events that are coming to them,” Mander said.

Advertisement

“We’re going to make it work. There is a lot to work to be done, but we’re going to make it work.” Start the day with a summary of the day’s most important and interesting stories, analysis and insights. Sign up for our Morning Edition newsletter.

title: Third-degree burns and skin grafts: The popular medical device that left a trail of injured children
text:
Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

An investigation into medical devices reveals alarming flaws in both the machines we put in our bodies and the system that oversees them. See all stories .

We were in the trenches. My seven-month-old baby had croup and his little body rattled through the night as he barked like a seal. Like many desperate parents wanting a solution, I drove to the nearest pharmacy. In the third aisle, I thought I’d found the solution: a bulky, plastic humidifier called the Euky Bear. Age reporter Henrietta Cook was burnt using the Euky Bear. Simon Schluter An image of a blonde, sleeping baby graced the cardboard packaging, alongside the words “natural all-night relief from the symptoms of cold and flu”. I never experienced that relief. Instead, it became a powerful case study that illustrated failures with the sales and oversight of medical devices – the very issues currently being exposed by this masthead’s Medical Malfunctions investigation. The $58 device didn’t have an off button, and I worried it would overheat and start a fire once all the water had evaporated. I was also left with a large, painful burn on my chest after my skin brushed the humidifier’s hot plastic while I was trying to turn it off one night. It took months to fade. I was one of the lucky ones.

Advertisement

Data scraped off the Therapeutic Goods Administration’s website as part of this masthead’s investigation into the toll of medical devices shows that Australians have lodged 55 adverse reports about the Euky Bear since 2021. At least two babies required skin grafts to treat third-degree burns after touching the device. “Infant suffered a full thickness burn to two fingers requiring a skin graft,” one 2022 report from a consumer read. In another incident, a parent reported that their Euky Bear had overheated and smoked out their baby’s bedroom with a “dangerous, plastic smell”. That baby was rushed to hospital after inhaling the smoke. For years, the company received a steady stream of complaints about its device. Dozens of disgruntled customers took to the Euky Bear Facebook page to report their terrifying experiences. One mother wrote that her vaporiser had started to smoke and almost caused a fire. Many raised concerns about the residue in the base of their vaporiser after use, and believed this was linked to the heating element disintegrating.

Advertisement

In September 2023, about 10 months after my dangerous encounter with the vaporiser, the company issued a partial recall of four batches of the Euky Bear. It said this was due to a “quality issue” that could lead to the heating element overheating. My device was not among the four batches of the EBSV2013 model of the vaporiser that had been recalled. I’m not someone who puts up with shoddy products, so I fired off an email to the manufacturer, Felton Grimwade & Bosisto’s Pty Ltd. In the October 6, 2023 email, I asked for a refund and explained that the device had burnt me. If it had burnt me, what sort of damage might it inflict on a young child? I also sounded the alarm about black sediment which accumulated at the bottom of the device after each use. “It is coming from the heating module which appears to be disintegrating,” I wrote. The pharmacy where I purchased the device arranged for it to be sent to the manufacturer for safety testing.

Advertisement

A month later, the manufacturer emailed me to say that the device was “operating per normal”. “We are confident that the vaporiser does not pose a risk, if used in accordance with the operating manual,” the customer services team wrote. They informed me that it would report my concerns to the Therapeutic Goods Administration. Loading I sent a quick response: “Thank you for running these tests on the machine. Unfortunately I will never feel safe using it again after my experience.” The Euky Bear remained in my garage, gathering dust, and I didn’t think of it again until an image of the device flashed up on my colleague Liam Mannix’s computer screen at work earlier this year. We were investigating medical devices, and our web scrapers were busily making sense of thousands of adverse event reports made to the TGA. This device had caught Mannix’s attention because its manufacturers had issued the partial recall of a device model, only to issue a full recall of the same model 18 months later.

Advertisement

We wondered how often that happened and if the device manufacturer was trying to minimise the damage with a partial recall before the tide of adverse reports became too much to ignore. Related Article Exclusive

Medical Malfunctions ‘Race to the bottom’: Investigation reveals toll of weak medical device laws Mannix’s sleuthing also alerted me that my Euky Bear, which had previously been declared safe by the manufacturers, had since been recalled. The company – despite having my email – never contacted me to tell me the device was now unsafe for sale. The recall notice on the TGA website, published in May 2025, spoke to many of the concerns raised by customers. The device’s heating module could disintegrate, produce smoke, and the exposed element could cause severe burns. Consumers were told to stop using the device, cut its power cord and dispose of it. While Euky Bear’s sponsor also cancelled its registration in March 2026, I was still able to purchase the device online almost two months later. On May 4, I bought another Euky Bear from Dick Smith, which was selling the device on behalf of a company called Cosmetics Squad. While this second humidifier was a newer model not caught up in the recalls, it should have never been sold because it was no longer registered as a medical device. It is a criminal offence to supply, sell or import unregistered medical devices in Australia. The offence carries financial penalties and even imprisonment.

title: Port guide: Papeete, French Polynesia
text:
Advertisement

InspirationCruises Port guide: Papeete, French Polynesia Brian Johnston July 20, 2026 — 11:39am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

This article is part of Traveller’s ultimate guide to cruise ports. See all stories .

This modest port may be drab and unexciting, but it’s the gateway to one of the world’s most stunning, must-visit cruise regions. Who goes there Papeete is visited by the larger ships of companies such as Norwegian Cruise Line, Princess Cruises and Royal Caribbean during extended South Pacific journeys. It’s also the departure and arrival port for French Polynesia cruises operated by luxury lines such as Ponant, Silversea and Windstar. Mixed freight-passenger company Aranui and Paul Gauguin (now owned by Ponant) are locally based. Sail on in Papeete – the gateway to one of the world’s most stunning, must-visit cruise regions. iStock

Advertisement

On a French Polynesia cruise, you might well arrive in the dark following a short hop from Moorea. During the sail away, however, the steep green mountains that rise behind Papeete and the lagoon that fronts it redeem the town. That said, you’ll be getting far more spectacular approaches and departures at every other island. Sign up for the Traveller Deals newsletter Get exclusive travel deals delivered straight to your inbox. Sign up now. Berth rites Te Anuanua Cruise Terminal, opened in early 2025, has brought organisation and polish to a formerly dishevelled experience. It has ample seating, luggage storage, and stalls selling handicrafts. Wander upstairs to the cultural space and exhibition room to see what’s happening. If you’re peckish, the surrounding areas have several restaurants. Before and after Travellers will likely have to overnight in Papeete before their cruise. Resort hotels sit on the edge of the lagoon beyond town. Hilton Hotel Tahiti has an agreeable lagoon-style pool and views towards Moorea and because it lies between the town and airport, is favoured by cruise companies. InterContinental Tahiti Resort & Spa is also close to the airport, but further from town. There are cheaper but uninspiring hotel options in Papeete itself.

Advertisement

Going ashore Papeete’s town centre is full of markets and shops. French Polynesia is ravishing, but its gateway capital unfortunately anything but – and hot and humid too. Only the coconut palms and bougainvillea soften its edges. Still, the waterfront is pleasant, and the town full of markets and shops. If you’re after Polynesian black pearls, this is your opportunity. Those with more time should explore beyond town. Don’t miss The Museum of Tahiti and the Islands, 15 kilometres from Papeete, is the best museum in French Polynesia and has sumptuous views across the strait towards Moorea from extensive gardens. It has exhibits on geology, flora, fauna and handicrafts, and you can learn more about customs such as tattooing and tapa-weaving. It also does a good job of explaining Polynesia’s pre-European history and the impact of colonialisation.

Advertisement

Related Article Tahiti There’s more to this paradise than just overwater bungalows Get active Tu Marama Promenade links several squares and parks along the waterfront. You’ll pass Paofai Gardens, which has outdoor exercise equipment. Otherwise, you can jet ski or sail in the harbour. Tahiti’s rugged interior is splendid, but you’ll need a full day for four-wheel-drive tours that might include rappelling down the side of waterfalls or crawling through lava tubes. Best bites Papeete residents are fond of food trucks or roulottes and, given the high cost of dining, so are visitors. You’ll find several outside the cruise terminal, and more along the waterfront. They might serve anything from coffee and firi firi (the local doughnut) to full meals. They generally offer Polynesian, French or Chinese cuisine. Two dishes to try are pua’a roti (glazed roast pork) and poisson cru (raw fish marinated in lime juice and coconut milk). Most roulottes are cash only.

Advertisement

Further afield The most popular excursion takes cruise passengers on a drive around the road that circumnavigates most of the island. You’ll stop in places such as Matavai Bay for the view, Point Venus associated with Captain James Cook, and the Water Gardens of Vaipahi. Alternatively, a four-wheel-drive excursion will take you into the lush Papenoo Valley, which has archaeological sites and waterfalls.

title: Public service to get AI guardrails for fairer system
text:
Artificial intelligence used in automated decision-making by the public service and federal agencies will face new rules under a national plan designed to make the system fairer and more transparent.

The federal government has revealed a set of AI safety priorities that touch on privacy, consumer protections and developing a framework for automated decision-making.

It comes after Prime Minister Anthony Albanese's major speech last week on the government's plan to introduce AI policy guardrails.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

The public service and Australia's agencies are increasingly using AI for automated decision making as advocates raise concerns the emerging technology could displace human oversight.

Industry Minister Tim Ayres says AI must be used to provide better and fairer services. (Bianca De Marchi/AAP PHOTOS)

Industry Minister Tim Ayres said Attorney-General Michelle Rowland will be working across government to make sure there's transparency and a clear code of practice that applies right across the public service.

"What we want to make sure that's done in a way where there's always a person and responsibility in the chain of decision making, where it's providing better and fairer services for Australians," he told ABC Radio on Monday.

"Australians can have confidence when they're dealing with government, they're getting a better service and they're getting a fairer service."

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Labor will also be looking to make sure that the laws, regulators and institutions are effectively protecting consumers.

"AI safety, in particular, in the workplace ... consumer law and retail surveillance pricing. All of those areas are impacted potentially by artificial intelligence," Senator Ayres said.

"This is the Albanese government making sure that artificial intelligence is safe for Australians, and that its adoption is in the national interest."

The illegal and flawed robodebt scheme unveiled shortcomings in the use of automated processes.

Asked if the scheme had demonstrated the need for human intervention, Senator Ayres said the government wanted to ensure the technologies were being applied fairly.

Anthony Albanese delivered a major policy speech on AI regulations in Sydney. (Dan Himbrechts/AAP PHOTOS)

"That's about using artificial intelligence in our interest, in the national interest, but also making sure that Australians can have faith and confidence in this technology, and they understand that government is taking responsibility for delivering these services to all Australians fairly and in the national interest," he said.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Digital Rights Watch welcomed the AI plan, with its founder Lizzie O'Shea saying privacy needed to be prioritised.

"Australia's privacy law has undergone a years-long review and the government has already agreed to a number of reforms," she said.

"It is high time these were implemented, so we are pleased to see this as a priority focus for the government in the context of AI."
